Brookline Select Board Meeting - May 12, 2026

Select Board · Brookline · 2026-05-12
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

During the Brookline Select Board meeting on May 12, 2026, members discussed several key items including the nomination of a new vice chair, the importance of condemning anti-Semitism, and plans for a contest awards ceremony scheduled for May 17. The Board voted to approve a memorandum of agreement regarding the $150,000 funding to support local nonprofits and another vote to raise fines to $50 for various infractions, effective July 1, 2026. The afternoon also saw discussions on the importance of public safety in regard to infrastructure and commercial development, alongside community engagement in its planning ([19:54], [70:03], [199:44], [202:19], [263:44]).

14:07 Good evening. I'm Bernard Green, still

14:09 chair of the Brookline Select Board.

14:12 This is our regular meeting for May.

14:15 What do you mean no? We haven't elected

14:18 anyone to replace me yet. Any rate, I'm

14:20 gonna assume that I still have. um for

14:23 May 12, 2026.

14:26 Um I'd like to start off by uh beginning

14:29 the reorganization of the select board.

14:31 Thank you. Um so this is the

14:34 organizational meeting of the board. Uh

14:35 once again, I have the privilege to

14:37 serve theoretically for a very brief

14:39 period as chair prom while I read these

14:41 cards to you. Um at this the board's

14:44 first meeting after the town election. I

14:46 first like to call the meeting to order

14:47 and direct your attention to the first

14:49 item on the agenda which calls for the

14:50 board to organize with the election of a

14:52 chair. I am to read the results of the

14:54 election for select board. Uh the

14:57 results are uh John Vanskoak 5,864

15:02 Anthony Buano 7,593

15:06 Amanda El Zimmerman 9,225.

15:10 The results of which being that Dr.

15:11 Zimmerman and Mr. Buo are elected select

15:13 board members. We thank Mr. Vanco Skoyak

15:15 for his service to the board. Um, we

15:17 will now proceed with the election of a

15:19 chair. Nominations are in order and no

15:21 seconds are required. Do I have a

15:23 nomination?

15:23 Yes.

15:25 Uh, I'd like to make a nomination, but

15:26 first I'd like to read a statement.

15:30 I've served as chair since May 2019

15:34 until today with a break from May 2022

15:38 to February 2023. So, I've been chair of

15:41 the select board for a total of six

15:44 years and three months. They included

15:47 some very, very tough times, including

15:49 the pandemic and the national outrage

15:52 over the killing of George Floyd in

15:54 Minneapolis. The period included a time

15:57 when my in my opinion unwarranted

16:00 comparisons of the Brooklyn Police

16:01 Department with Minneapolis and other

16:03 police departments where killings and

16:05 abuse of black residents occurred.

16:08 unwarranted because the comparisons

16:11 failed to acknowledge 30 plus years of

16:14 effort of this select board under the

16:17 leadership originally of Marty Rosenthal

16:20 and Jeffrey Allen and others working

16:22 with former police chief Daniel Olirri

16:25 to turn the Brookline Police Department

16:27 into a communityoriented and highly

16:30 trained department that had extensive

16:32 guard rails that minimized the risk of

16:35 political or police abuse. The critics

16:38 of the police saw themselves as helpless

16:40 and assumed that because they couldn't

16:41 do it, it couldn't be done. But it was

16:44 done. Among the consequences of this

16:47 period was that Brookline was

16:48 dangerously without a permanent police

16:50 chief and top leadership of the police

16:53 department for 30 months. Starting in

16:56 July of 2020 when Chief Andy Lipson

16:59 resigned and continuing until March 2023

17:04 when Chief Jen Pastor was appointed

17:06 permanent police chief. This includes a

17:09 short embarrassing period in 2022 when

17:13 Chief Ashley Gonzalez was in command

17:15 until being quickly dismissed for

17:18 inappropriate uh conduct.

17:22 In addition, the anti- police fervor

17:25 resulted in the ending of the school

17:26 resource officer program in the schools

17:29 and the walk-in talk officers in the

17:31 housing authority. two programs that

17:33 were successful in disrupting what was

17:36 widely called the school to prison

17:38 pipeline in Brooklyn by creating

17:40 relationships with youth and finding

17:43 alternatives to the criminal justice

17:44 system when youth did what youth do as

17:48 we all did um and found themselves in

17:52 trouble. John Vancoyak and I were the

17:54 two votes opposing that big mistake.

17:57 It's been an exciting and challenging

17:58 six years and three months and I thank

18:00 the select board and town administrators

18:02 during this period for entrusting me

18:04 with that leadership. I don't claim that

18:07 it has been a that I have been a perfect

18:10 chair. But to paraphrase Grace Jones, I

18:13 may not have been perfect, but I may

18:15 have been perfect for Brooklyn. With

18:18 that, I'd like to nominate as chair new

18:20 chair of the select board, David

18:22 Pearlman.

18:24 Thank you, Mr. Green. Are there any

18:26 other nominations?

18:28 There being none, is there a motion that

18:31 I move closure of the nominations.

18:33 Excellent. I will take a vote on the nom

21:01 Anthony

21:02 I.

21:02 I'm sorry. Michael

21:04 And I also vote I. So congratulations

21:07 Michael.

21:09 Thank you very much.

21:12 back.

21:15 I thought that came with the chair

21:17 position though.

21:17 No, no, it comes with a sleepy person.

21:22 With that, we will now move to

21:25 announcements and updates.

21:27 Uh, I would like to begin by addressing

21:33 a situation that occurred at uh,

21:35 Michael's Deli. I know there's been a

21:38 lot of conversation about this both on

21:40 the town meeting members list serve.

21:41 There was an event at Michael's deli um

21:45 on Friday afternoon that I know that

21:47 many people at this table were able to

21:49 attend and some other community members

21:51 also some u recent past select board

21:54 members were at that uh the anti-semitic

21:58 vandalism at Michael's Deli deserves

22:01 unequivocal condemnation. Defacing a

22:04 photograph of a Jewish family was not

22:07 legitimate protest or protected

22:09 political expression. It was a

22:11 destructive act targeting Jewish

22:13 identity in a profoundly personal and

22:16 hateful way. Criticism of any

22:19 government, including the government of

22:21 Israel, can never justify singling out

22:24 Jewish people, Jewishowned businesses,

22:27 or Jewish symbols for intimidation or

22:30 harassment.

22:32 To direct attacks at someone because

22:34 they are Jewish is anti-Semitic. Period.

22:37 In a community like Brooklyn, where

22:39 diversity and mutual respect are core

22:41 values, there must be no tolerance for

22:44 acts that attempt to intimidate or

22:47 dehumanize members of the Jewish

22:49 community. Brookline should stand firmly

22:51 with Michael's Deli, its owners, staff,

22:54 and patrons, and reject the

22:56 normalization of anti-Semitic conduct

22:59 disguised as activism. Hate directed at

23:02 Jews because they are Jews is hate

23:05 regardless of the political language

23:08 used to rationalize it. I want to thank

23:10 the dozens of civic leaders in our

23:13 community who have already denounced the

23:15 anti-semitic vandalism at Michael's

23:17 Delhi, including elected leaders in

23:19 their individual capacities, various

23:22 leaders from faith communities as well.

23:24 The outpouring of support for Michael's

23:26 Delhi, not just from members of the

23:28 Jewish community, but also from secular

23:30 leaders, Christians, Muslims, and other

23:33 religious groups, leaves me with

23:35 optimism that we will stand together as

23:37 a broader community against hatred in

23:40 every form.

23:43 Now, uh, Lisa Shatz, a town meeting

23:45 member in precinct 11, has been

23:48 circulating a petition on the town

23:51 meeting list serve that I would like to

23:52 read. Brooklyn condemns anti-semitism.

23:56 We, the undersigned Brooklyn town

23:58 meeting members and town leaders,

23:59 condemn the repeated targeting and

24:01 intimidation of Jewishowned businesses

24:04 and Jewish residents in our community.

24:06 Brooklyn should remain a place where

24:08 residents of all backgrounds feel safe

24:11 and respected. Political disagreement

24:14 must never become harassment or

24:16 intimidation directed at people because

24:19 they are Jewish. Now I'm not sure what

24:21 the precise number is in terms of who

24:24 has signed on to this so far and

24:27 obviously due to open meeting law we as

24:29 a board were not able to discuss in

24:31 advance whether we too would endorse

24:34 this statement or even our thoughts on

24:36 this as a board. I know this leaves many

24:38 in the community frustrated when

24:40 sometimes select board seems a bit slow

24:43 in issuing a unified statement but we

24:46 are prohibited by law from having

24:49 discussions even in those situations if

24:51 it's a deliberation. So that's why I've

24:53 prepared my individual statement. Paul

24:55 Warren who's here in the audience was

24:56 very prompt in issuing a statement of

24:58 his own while he was on the board and

25:01 many others have uh issued statements. I

25:04 would recommend that uh this board also

25:07 endorse uh Lisa Shatz's statement.

25:10 That's not technically before us today

25:12 as a docket item, so we may need to take

25:15 a little more time to approve that, but

25:17 I certainly share the sentiments that

25:19 she expressed in her statement. Uh

25:23 something else I would like to bring up

25:25 briefly, we had a uh farewell for Paul

25:29 Warren and his last select board meeting

25:31 where each of us were able to speak

25:33 about his contributions and what we

25:36 enjoyed about working with him. We did

25:38 not afford John Vancoy that uh same

25:42 farewell opportunity because we weren't

25:45 100% sure of course about whether he

25:47 would still be here today or not. So, I

25:50 want to echo the town administrator's

25:52 sentiment uh that he stated earlier in

25:55 thanking John for his uh six years of

25:59 service to the select board and for his

26:01 decades of service to our community. I'm

26:05 confident we haven't heard the last of

26:07 him. I hope we haven't. Uh he has put in

26:09 a lot of effort and work into helping

26:11 make Brookline what it is today. and I

26:14 look forward to his uh continued

26:16 contributions along with those of Paul

26:19 Warren who's here today and clearly

26:21 misses us already.

26:25 And with that, I would like to open the

26:27 floor to others who may have some

26:29 updates or announcements to make.

26:30 Michael,

26:32 thank you very much. Um I have uh three

26:36 uh sets of announcements to make. Um the

26:39 first one is on behalf of the Brooklyn

26:41 AsianAmerican

26:43 Family Network. Um the BAFN

26:47 uh is hosting their 11th annual essay

26:51 contest awards ceremony. Um that is

26:54 happening tomorrow, Wednesday, May 13th,

26:57 uh at 6 o'clock p.m. at the Coolage

26:59 Corner Theater. Um and all members of

27:02 the public are warmly invited to attend.

27:08 Um the second uh announcement that I

27:11 have uh I'd like to recognize two

27:14 important obser observances celebrated

27:16 together this month. Uh Jewish American

27:18 Heritage Month and Aaron Fstein Day. On

27:23 Sunday, May 17th from 2:30 to 4:00 p.m.

27:27 the community is invited to gather in

27:29 room 103 at Brooklyn Town Hall for an

27:33 afternoon of learning and music in honor

27:35 of Aaron Fstein.

27:37 The event will feature nationally

27:39 recognized educator Yavila McCoy who

27:42 will speak about the Jewish value of

27:46 loving kindness and its importance in

27:48 our lives today. The program will also

27:51 include music from the Kles encounters

27:54 band and light refreshments. Erin

27:56 Fstein, a Brooklyn native and CEO of

28:00 Malden Mills, whose remarkable act of

28:02 conscience and compassion, paying his

28:05 workers salaries and rebuilding his

28:07 factory after a devastating fire,

28:10 continues to inspire people well beyond

28:13 Brookline. This event during Jewish

28:16 American Heritage Month offers an

28:18 opportunity for all of us to celebrate

28:20 Aaron's legacy and the many

28:22 contributions of Jewish Americans to our

28:25 communities and our country. So I

28:28 encourage residents to attend and join

28:30 in this meaningful community

28:33 celebration.

28:35 And thirdly, um though I know we are uh

28:38 reading a proclamation uh in celebration

28:41 of DPW week uh shortly in our agenda, um

28:45 I would like to point out two uh

28:49 specific uh events relating to DPW week.

28:53 Um the first is the DPW is holding the

28:57 DPW public forum, an evening with the

29:00 commissioner and DPW leadership team on

29:03 Wednesday, May 20th from 6 o'clock to

29:06 8:00 p.m. Uh it is a Zoom meeting that

29:09 you can sign up at

29:10 tinyurl.comdpw-pub-forum-2026.

29:18 This forum is intended to provide town

29:20 meeting members, elected officials, and

29:23 interested residents with an opportunity

29:25 to hear directly from DPW leadership,

29:28 ask questions ahead of town meeting, and

29:31 learn more about current projects,

29:33 services, and priorities. I know from my

29:36 own experience, this has been a very

29:38 valuable opportunity to ask questions

29:41 and hear directly from the commissioner

29:43 and her leadership team. So I invite you

29:46 to join all of DPW and share this

29:49 information amongst your networks. And

29:52 secondly, uh DPW day is on Tuesday, May

29:56 19th. Um there is a public uh

30:00 opportunity to join DPW from noon to

30:03 5:00 pm on May 19th at the annual DPW

30:07 day at the municipal service center at

30:10 870 Hammond Street. This free,

30:13 familyfriendly, community event is open

30:15 to the public from noon to 5:00 pm and

30:18 is a wonderful opportunity to meet our

30:20 crews, explore equipment up close, and

30:23 see firsthand the working that goes into

30:25 keeping Brookline running. So, I invite

30:29 everyone to enjoy DPW Day as well.

30:34 All right. While we're on the theme of

30:36 cultural appreciation, I want to mention

30:38 that this is also Asian-American and

30:41 Pacific Islander Heritage Month. And as

30:44 part of that celebration, there is an

30:48 event tomorrow evening at the Koolage

30:51 Corner Theater, which is sponsored by

30:53 Baffin, the Brooklyn Asian-American

30:55 Family Network, and they will be

30:57 convening the 11th annual essay contest

31:00 award ceremony. Uh so for those of you

31:02 who can attend again tomorrow 6 pm

31:04 Kulage Corner Theater, I highly

31:06 recommend it. Uh I also want to thank

31:11 Bernard Green for his six years, three

31:13 months of service as chair of the select

31:17 board. You did an excellent job. I uh

31:20 very much enjoyed uh mentoring under you

31:23 for the uh last year as your vice chair.

31:26 I learned a lot from you. Uh, and I also

31:30 want to congratulate our two newest

31:32 members of the select board, Amanda

31:34 Zimmerman and Anthony Wono. Amanda, this

31:37 is the first time I have a female

31:39 colleague on the board. Uh, so you're

31:40 really a trailblazer, at least on my

31:42 time on this board, and also the highest

31:44 vote getter in select board history, at

31:47 least in modern history, as far as we

31:49 know. Very impressive.

31:53 and Anthony, you're just 26 years old

31:56 and elected to select board. As recently

31:59 as a couple weeks ago, I was the baby of

32:01 the board and now I'm middleaged. I've

32:04 got two who are younger than me, so

32:06 times are changing. So, congratulations

32:08 to you both.

32:09 Thank you.

32:14 Anyone else?

32:15 Yeah.

32:15 All right, Bernard. And then we'll go to

32:16 Amanda. Go ahead.

32:17 Okay. I I have three things I'd like to

32:19 mention. I was also at the um event at

32:22 Michael's Deli that uh Scott Gladstone

32:25 organized. Um and yeah, I talked to

32:29 Stephen PJovich

32:31 um who had an interesting perspective on

32:34 what was going on and I asked him if I

32:36 could use his his uh quote and he said

32:40 yes. Um he said put stickers on other

32:43 businesses and on mine but not just on

32:46 mine because that is not how we have a

32:48 discussion. We may disagree, but at

32:51 least we should be talking. It's not

32:53 okay to single out just my business

32:55 because that is not showing you want to

32:58 discuss the issue. Rough paraphrase, but

33:01 the substance is what he said. Um, you

33:04 know, the right to free speech is

33:05 important and gives us the right to

33:07 discuss and disagree. It does not give

33:09 anyone the right to threaten or bully or

33:11 intimidate, which was what I think

33:13 happened at um, Michaels. No one should

33:16 be shocked that anti-semitism or other

33:19 forms of hatred, whether racism,

33:21 anti-gay and anti-trans, etc., exist in

33:24 Brooklyn. We should investigate and

33:27 prosecute vandalism under uh state law,

33:30 uh, which, you know, has serious

33:32 consequences for such vandalism. But we

33:35 also must learn and teach others,

33:37 including those foolishly vandalizing,

33:40 how to have a respectful conversation

33:42 about hot button issues, even hot button

33:45 issues. The people who vandalize only

33:48 Michael's Deli with the stickers

33:50 stickers may fear such a discussion, but

33:53 we can't hide from it. Um, so that's

33:56 that's one comment. Um

34:00 secondly,

34:02 um I'd like to uh mention that uh Iana

34:05 Lindo Smith is uh retiring. Uh she is uh

34:10 head of community development at the

34:12 planning department and I know her

34:14 mainly through the CBD CDBG advisory

34:18 committee. Um, and you know, a little

34:22 little statement here uh that was uh

34:25 help that uh um Cara Brudin helped me

34:28 put together. For more than four

34:30 decades, Iana Lindo Smith has been an an

34:33 inspiring presence in the town of

34:35 Brooklyn. Over 41 years, 41. Wow.

34:41 41 years of dedicated service. Her work

34:44 her work has left a lasting impression

34:46 on the community reflecting not only

34:48 deep professional commitment but also a

34:51 genuine care for the people and

34:53 nonprofit institutions her work in

34:55 community development has supported. I

34:58 worked with her on the CDBG advisory

35:01 committee for a number of years and her

35:02 hard work behind the scenes made it

35:06 possible for us to use this federal CDBG

35:10 money uh to benefit communities that we

35:13 could not as a select board directly uh

35:16 fund uh with town resources um such as

35:20 the capital and other needs of the

35:22 Brookline Housing Authority. the the

35:24 money that we used from CDBG made a huge

35:28 difference in the lives of people in the

35:30 housing authority. Um her tenure stands

35:34 as a testament to consistency,

35:36 integrity, and adaptability.

35:38 Through challenging times and evolving

35:40 community needs, Iana remained a

35:43 reliable force, someone colleagues and

35:46 residents alike could trust. Long-term

35:49 service of this kind is rare and it

35:51 speaks volumes about both her love for

35:53 her work, her joy is work in working

35:56 with her colleagues and her belief in

35:58 public service. Beyond the day-to-day

36:01 responsibilities, her legacy is found in

36:03 the relationship she built, the

36:05 knowledge she shared, and the example

36:07 she set for others. She has helped shape

36:10 the future culture of the work and the

36:12 culture of the workforce and contributed

36:14 to the stability that makes local

36:16 government effective. She's a reminder

36:19 of the value of dedication and the

36:21 difference one individual can make over

36:24 time and she made it over 41 years. I it

36:27 just blows my mind knowing her, you

36:29 know, doesn't seem like she's been

36:31 around that long, but 41 years is quite

36:33 amazing. Uh so anyway, thank you. uh and

36:38 um you know I think she's going to be

36:39 here later on when we have the CDBG

36:42 discussion and we can uh all thank her

36:45 at that point too. Um we're you

36:49 mentioned a few things about John

36:51 Vancoyak. I'd like to just say a couple

36:53 things. Um John and I didn't always

36:56 agree. Many times we did agree and the

36:59 times we agreed were times when both he

37:01 and I were sort of going against the

37:04 grain on on issues. And I appreciated

37:06 that about John. Um, and maybe it's why

37:09 he's not here. You want to get some

37:11 grain on some issues. But, you know, he

37:14 was an important person on the select

37:16 board even when I disagreed with him

37:18 because he opened up my mind to things

37:20 that maybe I was overlooking or not

37:23 paying or not seeing as as important as

37:26 I should have. So, uh, we'll miss John.

37:28 Uh but we uh will not um um or he will

37:32 not u disappear from uh Brook town of

37:36 Brooklyn and select board's uh work. So

37:40 Amanda,

37:41 I just wanted to say something really

37:42 briefly that the town clerk uh said when

37:45 we were being sworn in, which is our

37:47 last election was the highest turnout

37:49 that Brooklyn has seen since World War

37:51 II. Um or maybe World War I. Ben will

37:54 graduate if I'm wrong. Um which I think

37:57 is phenomenal. It just speaks to the

38:01 town voters. Um, and so we should all

38:04 hope that we can even beat that number

38:06 next year. So,

38:09 what else? Oh, go ahead.

38:10 Sorry. One more quick thing. Um, town

38:12 hall, uh, the public health building and

38:15 the main branch of the public library

38:16 here will be closed on Thursday the

38:19 14th. Um, that's for staff professional

38:22 development day. This is a first for the

38:24 town. Um, we've had staff appreciation

38:26 day before, but we've never had the

38:28 opportunity to actually take time for

38:30 the whole team, especially folks who

38:31 have to ordinarily work at the desks and

38:33 so forth, to do some training, um, to do

38:35 some betterment so that we can better

38:36 serve the community. Um, it's really

38:38 rare to have that opportunity. Um, we

38:41 want to thank the community. We

38:42 understand it is a hardship when we have

38:44 to close operations, but we want to be

38:45 thankful to you all for your patience on

38:48 this. If you have business with the town

38:50 that you need to come in for, I would

38:51 suggest you come in on Wednesday, which

38:52 will be regular working hours. Friday,

38:55 which again is early closure, but it's

38:56 regular working hours. So, um, just want

38:59 to let everyone know again that is Town

39:00 Hall, that is the public health

39:02 building, and that is the public library

39:04 here in Brooklyn Village. So, again,

39:07 thank you all for your patience and your

39:08 forbearance, and thank you to the staff.

39:10 Um, it's a real rare opportunity to be

39:12 able to do this. We're very grateful for

39:14 it.

39:16 All right. And with that, we will now

39:19 move to public comment. Melanie, if

39:21 you'd like to go over the roles.

39:22 Sure.

39:23 Thank you for joining us for public

39:24 comment. This is an opportunity for us

39:26 to hear your perspective on the issues

39:28 in Brookline that matter to you. Each

39:30 person speaking tonight is limited to

39:32 three minutes. You don't need to use the

39:34 entire time, but you may if you'd like.

39:37 Once 15 minutes has been met, there is

39:38 an opportunity at the conclusion of the

39:40 select board's business for additional

39:42 comments. Members of the public

39:44 sometimes raise questions during public

39:46 comment. We may be able to provide a

39:48 quick answer to a question, but are more

39:50 likely to work with staff to get a more

39:52 thorough answer and respond over email.

39:54 The onscreen timer will flash orange

39:56 when you have 30 seconds remaining and

39:58 red when your time is nearly up. Please

40:00 conclude your remarks at that time. If

40:03 you have more to say, you're welcome to

40:04 send an email to board members

40:06 expressing your thoughts in greater

40:07 detail. Any person wishing to speak must

40:10 begin their comment by identifying

40:12 themselves with their full name, either

40:14 their Brooklyn precinct number or street

40:16 address, and the specific topic on which

40:18 they wish to speak. The board encourages

40:20 but does not require that remote users

40:22 turn their video on when commenting.

40:26 So, we have three people signed up. Um

40:28 Paul Ser is first and Paul

40:32 Paul, I'll let you know when your 30

40:34 seconds is near. Okay.

40:36 Hopefully, you will not need to.

40:37 Hopefully, I'll be long done. Uh before

40:39 I um introduce myself formally, I want

40:42 to uh congratulate and thank Amanda and

40:45 Anthony for stepping up to the select

40:47 board. Uh and David Prman for elected

40:51 chair. Uh but most importantly, I want

40:53 to thank Bernard for serving for what I

40:56 believe, and I've been around a long

40:58 time, Bernard, a record six plus years.

41:00 I don't remember anyone serving that

41:01 long, never mind in the challenging

41:04 times that we've had recently. So um

41:08 thank you um for uh a nice transition.

41:12 Uh I want to speak very briefly about

41:15 one particular um uh sentence in uh the

41:23 tonight and it's in section 3B which um

41:28 is the section that references

41:30 increasing revenue through development

41:38 to explain why. So rather than, and

41:42 pardon me if I listen to my screen

41:43 reader,

41:49 rather than proactively seeking out

41:51 commercial development, I would suggest

41:53 a simple um amendment to that sentence

41:56 that says um uh seeking out um mixed

42:02 use. So insert mixed use including

42:05 commercial and then following that

42:08 insert in residential development

42:10 opportunities.

42:12 Why? Um two points. One first of all I

42:16 didn't introduce myself Paul Sander

42:18 precinct 13. I'm not speaking on behalf

42:20 of Brookline for everyone whose board

42:22 I'm on but I am reflecting on my

42:26 considerable experience um in a

42:28 leadership role on the economic

42:30 development advisory board. So um EDAB

42:33 has always prioritized

42:36 um achieving commercial development but

42:39 EDAB has also been practically

42:41 realistic. So I'll point out that the

42:43 three uh commercial property upzoning

42:47 since 2016

42:49 um which include um Emerald Island,

42:52 Waldo Durban in the proposal for Ches

42:55 Hill office park at Chestn Hill

42:56 commercial area rather. um those are all

42:59 mixeduse um developments with a

43:02 residential component. Those two phrases

43:04 mixeduse and residential don't presently

43:07 appear in the draft roadmap. The second

43:10 reason is that the road map itself says

43:13 that the um pending recommendations from

43:17 the um expenditure and revenue study

43:20 committee now having been delivered uh

43:23 to the select board should be considered

43:26 uh actually should be responded to.

43:28 30 seconds Paul.

43:30 Oh my god. So um uh they have um set

43:35 forth a development um memo to the

43:38 select board which they unanimously

43:40 voted and that memo says that commercial

43:43 development

43:45 uh should be on a mixeduse basis with

43:48 residential supporting commercial

43:50 development and in many cases making it

43:53 feasible. So in closing, I would like to

43:56 recommend that the select board consider

44:00 an important amendment reflective of

44:03 reality. Thank you.

44:10 Next we have Lisa Schutz.

44:20 Hello, I'm Lisa Shatz, precinct 11.

44:24 Members of the select board, I want to

44:26 speak briefly about why a strong public

44:29 response to anti-Semitic incidents

44:31 matters, not only morally, but

44:33 practically. History shows that hatred

44:36 rarely begins with violence. It begins

44:38 with words: intimidation, graffiti,

44:41 vandalism, and the normalization of

44:43 targeting a minority group. When those

44:46 acts are minimized or treated as

44:48 insignificant, the message received by

44:50 extremists is that the community will

44:52 tolerate escalation. We have seen this

44:54 pattern internationally.

44:57 In Bandi Beach, Australia, anti-Semitic

45:00 vandalism and harassment escalated over

45:03 time into increasing threatening public

45:05 incidents directed at Jews and Jewish

45:08 institutions. In London as well,

45:10 authorities and community leaders have

45:12 warned repeatedly that when anti-Semitic

45:15 rhetoric and intimidation are allowed to

45:17 become normalized in public life,

45:19 physical violence often follows. The

45:22 progression is predictable. Offensive

45:24 speech becomes harassment. Harassment

45:27 becomes vandalism. Vandalism becomes

45:29 violence. The point at which government

45:31 officials speak clearly and decisively

45:33 can interrupt that progression. That is

45:36 why public condemnation by elected

45:38 officials is not symbolic or

45:40 performative. It is preventative. It

45:43 establishes community standards. It

45:45 reassures targeted residents that they

45:48 are protected members of the community.

45:50 And it tells potential perpetrators that

45:52 Brooklyn will not look away. A strong

45:55 response does not require panic or

45:57 exaggeration. It simply requires moral

45:59 clarity. We do not wait for violence

46:02 before we speak up against hatred.

46:04 Brookline should remain a place where

46:05 Jewish residents and all residents know

46:08 that intimidation and anti-semitism will

46:11 be recognized early, condemned clearly,

46:13 and taken seriously. Thank you.

46:19 Next, we have Mike Sammon.

46:33 Uh, good evening. I'm Mike Sandman,

46:35 precinct 14, uh, 123 Fischer Avenue. Uh,

46:39 before I begin, uh, I want to say

46:41 congratulations to, um, to Amanda and to

46:46 Anthony and to David, and I'm sure you

46:49 will, um, even as a middle-aged member,

46:52 you'll you'll do just fine speaking to

46:54 you as the former grandfather of the

46:56 select board. And, uh, Bernard, thank

46:58 you very much for your service. It was a

47:02 with you. Um

47:05 so uh

47:08 Andy uh Andy Fischer and uh and uh

47:11 Marissa vote have introduced a proposed

47:14 condition of appropriation for the DPW's

47:17 budget that would require implementing

47:19 vision zero designs on streets that are

47:22 being repaved. and I chaired the

47:26 policy and I understand the desire to

47:28 see it uh implemented.

47:30 Um

47:32 Paul Warren will be offering a condition

47:35 of appropriation uh that res that

47:38 regards the DPW budget that I hope you

47:40 will support as a substitute for Andy

47:43 and and Marissa's uh uh proposal. Uh the

47:47 impetus for their proposal

47:49 uh is DPW's plan to resurface Harvard

47:52 Street in the same way as Beacon was

47:54 resurfaced and relined last year. The

47:57 proponents want a full-scale redesign

47:59 along the lines of what's been done for

48:01 Washington Street and they're hoping

48:03 that that's what the consequence will

48:05 be. But this is a classic example of the

48:08 perfect uh being the enemy of the good.

48:10 DPW sort of skim coats heavily patched

48:13 streets as a stop gap until funds become

48:16 available for full redesign uh and

48:18 reconstruction for a major street like

48:20 Harvard. That means applying for funding

48:22 through Mass Dot's transportation

48:24 improvement process um uh that planning

48:28 process. The tip is a five-year rolling

48:30 plan for street and highway work.

48:32 Meanwhile, repaving Harvard and other

48:34 streets including and included in the

48:36 vision zero p plan would have to be put

48:38 on hold or delayed.

48:41 Um waiting for the uncertain prospect of

48:44 getting tip funding for Harvard Street

48:46 and then going through the gauntlet of

48:47 redesign will delay any improvements for

48:50 many years. uh repaving streets and

48:53 improving lines like what Brookline what

48:55 DBW did on Beacon Street actually would

48:58 contribute to cyclist and safety

49:01 cyclists and pedestrian safety right

49:02 now. Riding on uneven streets like

49:05 Harvard is uncomfortable for both

49:06 drivers and cyclists and it's actually

49:08 dangerous. Um I uh my business partner

49:12 rode over an open seam on Babcock

49:13 Street, fell off his bike and sustained

49:15 a spiral fraction of his leg. and I

49:18 think of that whenever I'm biking on a

49:20 rough patch street. Um, I hope you will

49:23 uh enable the DPW to go forward with its

49:25 planning. Thank you.

49:30 We don't have anybody else.

53:11 Yes. Thank you.

53:14 Sorry, that's better.

53:21 Okay.

53:23 A proclamation of the Brooklyn Select

53:25 Board for National Public Works Week,

53:29 May 17 through 23rd, 2026,

53:34 rooted in service powered by community.

53:38 Whereas public works professionals focus

53:41 on infrastructure, facilities, and

53:43 services that are of vital importance to

53:46 sustainable and resilient communities

53:49 and to public health, high quality of

53:52 life, and well-being of the people of

53:54 the town of Brooklyn. And whereas these

53:57 infrastructure, facilities, and services

54:00 could not be provided without the

54:02 dedicated efforts of public works

54:04 employees at all levels of government

54:07 and the private sector who are

54:09 responsible for rebuilding, improving,

54:12 and protecting our nation's

54:14 transportation, water supply, parks and

54:17 open space, solid waste programs, and

54:21 other structures and facilities

54:23 essential for our citizens. And whereas

54:26 it is in the public interest for the

54:29 citizens, civic leaders, and children in

54:32 the town of Brooklyn to gain knowledge

54:34 of and to maintain an ongoing interest

54:37 and understanding of the importance of

54:40 public works and public works programs

54:43 in their respective communities. And

54:46 whereas the year 2026 marks the 66th

54:49 annual National Public Works Week

54:52 sponsored by the American Public Works

54:54 Association,

54:56 be it now National Public Works Week

55:00 resolved. We, the select board in the

55:03 town of Brooklyn, do hereby designate

55:05 the week May 17 through 23rd, 2026 as

55:10 National Public Works Week. I urge all

55:13 citizens to join with representatives of

55:16 the American Public Works Association

55:18 and government agencies in activities,

55:21 events, and ceremonies designed to pay

55:24 tribute to our public works

55:26 professionals, engineers, managers, and

55:29 employees, and to recognize the

55:31 substantial contributions they make to

55:34 protecting our national health, safety,

55:37 and advancing quality of life for all.

55:41 In witness thereof, we set our hands and

55:43 cause the seal of the state done in the

55:46 town of Brooklyn, Massachusetts, this

55:48 12th day of May, 2026 by the Brookline

55:52 Select Board.

55:55 Thank you for reading that proclamation,

55:56 Michael. And two other items I would

55:59 like to highlight are 4 and 4 I.

56:36 ODICR

56:38 and the Brooklyn Asian-American Family

56:40 Network in the amount of $8,000. This

56:43 grant will be used to build its capacity

56:45 to organize and support Asian-American

56:47 Native Hawaiian Pacific Islander

56:51 groups and community events, including

1:00:00 slide deck here um just to sort of

1:00:04 orient everyone um

1:00:09 about tonight's agenda items.

1:00:12 So, just to to recap, because it's been

1:00:14 a few weeks since the select board has

1:00:16 been discussing the set of warrant

1:00:18 articles, I just wanted to remind the

1:00:20 board about um special town meeting one

1:00:23 articles one and two. Um article one

1:00:26 proposes to amend the zoning bylaw to

1:00:28 incorporate the Chestnut Hill commercial

1:00:30 area commercial overlay zoning. And

1:00:32 article two um authorizes the select

1:00:35 board to enter into a memorandum of

1:00:37 agreement and tax certainty agreement

1:00:38 with city realy. And sort of related to

1:00:41 article two, there are two documents.

1:00:43 The memorandum of agreement with City

1:00:45 Realy and a tax certainty agreement um

1:00:47 regarding 12801330

1:00:49 Boilston Street, also known as the

1:00:51 Chestnut Hill Office Park, which City

1:00:52 Realy owns.

1:00:55 And so tonight in your select board

1:00:56 packet materials, there was um a a

1:00:59 proposed amended version of special town

1:01:01 meeting one article one. And those um

1:01:05 three edits are intended to reflect the

1:01:07 final language included in the

1:01:09 memorandum of agreement. And then

1:01:11 there's also a final version of the

1:01:13 memorandum of agreement and attached um

1:01:16 several exhibits attached to that. Uh

1:01:18 the final version of this the memorandum

1:01:21 of agreement was sent to the Slack board

1:01:23 earlier this afternoon. uh along with a

1:01:26 complete set of exhibits. Um the legal

1:01:28 descriptions of the parcels in 1280 1330

1:01:31 Boilson Street were not included in the

1:01:33 packet materials on Friday, but those

1:01:35 are now um I I believe maybe uploaded to

1:01:38 the packet and at least in your inboxes.

1:01:42 So tonight, um, oh, and just to to

1:01:44 recap, recent select board conversations

1:01:46 regarding special town meeting one. Uh,

1:01:49 in a April 14th and April 28th, the

1:01:52 select board held a public hearing, two

1:01:54 public hearings um on the proposed

1:01:56 warrant articles. On April 28th, the

1:01:59 select board voted favorable action on

1:02:01 both articles and the um vote was for an

1:02:06 amended version of the zoning which

1:02:08 incorporated

1:02:10 a broad set of um amendments that were a

1:02:12 reflection of uh contributions made from

1:02:15 select board member Michael Rubenstein.

1:02:17 It included the Janice Khan consensus

1:02:19 amendment, several proposed amendments

1:02:20 from Dick Banka, um and a few proposed

1:02:24 friendly amendments from staff as well.

1:02:28 So this evening uh we are uh

1:02:30 recommending and have presented to the

1:02:32 select board uh a couple of actions um

1:02:35 for you to undertake. So one would be

1:02:37 the uh to reconsider and amend special

1:02:40 town meeting one article one um with

1:02:43 those amendments to reflect the final

1:02:45 MOA language and the provisions in the

1:02:46 MOA.

1:02:47 Excuse me. I think you mean article two.

1:02:51 I'm sorry. Article two. Thank you.

1:02:57 voting to approve to ex so sorry so

1:02:59 voting approval to execute the

1:03:42 in your packets. Um the amendments to

1:03:45 the MOA uh incorporated the Janus Khan

1:03:49 amendments. There was the Janice Khan

1:03:50 consensus amendment that was made to the

1:03:52 proposed zoning and there were a

1:03:54 corresponding set of amendments specific

1:03:56 to the MOA um requirements. And so those

1:03:59 are summarized here and just a couple of

1:04:01 highlights. Um there'll be $150,000 for

1:04:05 retail support fund for Chesnut Hill

1:04:08 commercial area small businesses. Um

1:04:10 there are several incentives for

1:04:11 expediting the completion of building A,

1:04:13 which is the hotel and condo building

1:04:16 that City Realy is proposing. Um and

1:04:19 then there's some recommendations about

1:04:21 membership included in a future design

1:04:23 advisory team for city realies project.

1:04:26 Other amendments uh include adding some

1:04:29 extra protections for the language

1:04:31 regarding the um requirement about 2% of

1:04:33 the heart construction costs to go

1:04:35 towards uh for infrastructure

1:04:37 mitigation, public safety mitigation.

1:04:39 And then the the key difference and the

1:04:41 most substantive change since um the

1:04:45 materials the MOA was sent in your

1:04:47 packet late last week is that the final

1:04:50 version of the MOA modifies and provides

1:04:52 some clarification around the timing of

1:04:55 when the town will receive the

1:04:56 infrastructure and public safety

1:04:58 mitigation funding.

1:05:00 And then uh proposed vote sequence and

1:05:03 motion language for tonight. Um I think

1:05:06 it would be appropriate for the select

1:05:07 board to consider amending special time

1:05:10 meeting one article one and then um

1:05:13 considering um executing the MOA.

1:05:17 So I'm happy to

1:05:19 answer any questions.

1:05:21 Um excuse me Meredith. Thank you very

1:05:23 much for the for the summary. Um I just

1:05:26 wanted to confirm also um if we received

1:05:29 the executed signature pages from city

1:05:32 realy on the MOA. Yes, thank you for the

1:05:35 reminder. So, also forwarded to the

1:05:37 select board earlier this afternoon

1:05:40 with the final MOA is um the set of

1:05:42 signatures from city realy.

1:05:47 Anthony um I had a question related to

1:05:49 the 150,000 um which would be um used to

1:05:53 support the commercial businesses that

1:05:55 would operate here. Who would be doing

1:05:57 out that 150,000? I believe I read it

1:06:00 was a nonprofit in theou. I was

1:06:02 wondering if we had a spec specific uh

1:06:04 one in mind for that.

1:06:08 Yes, thank you for the question. So, uh

1:06:10 right now the MOA the language is a bit

1:06:12 broader with just a local nonprofit

1:06:14 organization. Um we are in conversation

1:06:16 with Janice Khan about um one of the

1:06:19 Chestnut Hill neighborhood associations

1:06:21 um that is a nonprofit would be the um

1:06:25 steward of those funds. Has the town put

1:06:28 any thought into um operating the fund

1:06:30 in itself through the planning

1:06:32 department or the economic uh or like

1:06:34 EDAB or something or another uh town

1:06:36 agency that could maybe do that instead

1:06:38 of a nonprofit?

1:06:40 So, we we did consider that. Um there's

1:06:43 certainly sort of the administrative

1:06:46 capacity considerations. There's also

1:06:48 some complexity. Um there could be some

1:06:51 anti-aid amendment considerations. Um,

1:06:54 but I think also just based on the

1:06:56 intent of these proposed amendments to

1:06:58 the MOA, the importance of having

1:07:01 neighborhood

1:07:03 input and um,

1:07:06 uh, kind of control over about how those

1:07:08 funds are directed in support of

1:07:09 businesses in their community is very

1:07:11 important. So given that, I think it is

1:07:13 more appropriate that a neighborhood

1:07:16 oriented local nonprofit be the the

1:07:18 steward of those funds.

1:07:19 Okay. Thanks. Yeah, just so folks are

1:07:22 aware, it's it's similar to the kind of

1:07:23 problem we had with ARPA where we can't

1:07:25 directly fund a lot of programs because

1:07:28 we're the government and that raises

1:07:29 unique concerns, but we can provide

1:07:31 funding to nonprofits who then have more

1:07:33 leeway to do to run programs based on

1:07:35 that.

1:07:36 Bernard,

1:07:37 yeah. Are there no businessoriented

1:07:39 nonprofits that could uh be used for

1:07:41 this purpose?

1:07:43 I mean, like a chamber of commerce type

1:07:46 organization?

1:07:48 Uh, great question. So, we we've had

1:07:49 conversations with both the Brookline

1:07:51 Community Foundation. They were unable

1:07:53 um to be the um administrator of this

1:07:57 program because they have to support

1:08:00 other local nonprofit organizations. So,

1:08:02 they couldn't um provide the grant

1:08:03 funding to for-profit businesses. And

1:08:05 then we did have a conversation with the

1:08:07 Charles River Chamber of Commerce who

1:08:09 had a a similar set of concerns about

1:08:10 the complexities in terms of um

1:08:13 administering and um uh giving out those

1:08:17 funds. So, um, that's sort of why we

1:08:19 landed on local nonprofit leaning

1:08:21 towards one of the neighborhood

1:08:23 organizations.

1:08:24 Is there some way to include business

1:08:26 sort of perspectives in the local

1:08:29 community organizations activities?

1:08:33 You're saying in terms of developing the

1:08:35 grant criteria for this $150,000

1:08:38 program?

1:08:39 Yes.

1:08:41 Yeah. That's certainly something that um

1:08:43 from the economic development staff

1:08:44 perspective, we can try to connect that

1:08:47 group with, you know, local business

1:08:49 organizations as they're finalizing the

1:18:05 or with Lars right now? Yeah.

1:18:10 I'm 78 years old and I uh skated at the

1:18:14 rink the day it opened in I think 1955.

1:18:19 And very very often afterwards, I don't

1:18:22 think I've ever missed a season except

1:18:24 when I was away at school. um in college

1:18:30 important to me, dear to my heart. Um a

1:18:33 lot of friendships over the years have

1:18:34 been developed from the rink. My

1:18:37 grandmother uh and grandfather who my

1:18:39 grandmother's a wonderful cook, so I

1:18:41 spent a lot of time with her as a

1:18:42 teenager and she lived on Rockwood

1:18:44 Street um just on the Brook on the

1:18:47 Boston side of the line. Um I don't know

1:18:50 if you're familiar with Rockwood. sort

1:18:52 of it's parallel to Avon. Avon is at the

1:18:56 bottom of the ski hill at Lars Anderson

1:18:59 and um there was a little right of way

1:19:02 through the woods that I could walk to

1:19:04 the rink and I spent just a lot of time

1:19:06 there. A lot of my relationships with

1:19:09 Brooklyn people although I was living I

1:19:11 grew up in Boston. and I lived in

1:19:12 Jamaica plane until 1968 when my parents

1:19:16 moved to Brooklyn. And then I became a

1:19:19 Brookline uh homeowner in uh 198 well 34

1:19:25 years ago, whenever that was, and

1:19:26 brought up my family here. My children

1:19:30 uh went to Baker School and I had a

1:19:32 daughter who went to Brooklyn High

1:19:34 School and she was on the first Brooklyn

1:19:36 High School girls, if I can say that,

1:19:39 women's hockey team. Um, uh, I played,

1:19:44 uh, hockey as a youngster and through

1:19:47 high school and as an adult. Um, another

1:19:50 son, another daughter who all played

1:19:52 hockey. Although my interest isn't

1:19:54 really that enthusiastically based on

1:19:57 hockey. Um, I see that as a sort of

1:20:00 remote adjunct to what Lars Anderson

1:20:03 rink serves to the public and to the

1:20:05 Brooklyn residents. Um, I don't want to

1:20:07 certainly don't want to overemphasize

1:20:17 was very important to them. Am I getting

1:20:19 anywhere in the realm of what

1:20:21 That's great. I just wanted to hear a

1:20:26 Yeah.

1:20:27 Bernard,

1:20:27 so do you have any ideas or concerns

1:20:30 about the design particularly since

1:20:34 suitability for hockey?

1:20:37 Sure. Um, I don't think I'm really deep

1:20:39 enough into it yet. Um, in terms of the

1:20:44 certainly in my own mind deep into a

1:20:47 certain a couple of issues that I think

1:20:51 which is I would not like to see the

1:20:56 and it's limited but beautiful as to

1:20:59 what it is. Um, I personally am of a

1:21:03 minimalist style of of u uh approach.

1:21:09 Uh, I don't like to see things overdone.

1:21:11 I don't like to see money wasted. I

1:21:12 don't think it's necessary at the rink.

1:21:14 I think it's important to recognize the

1:21:17 rink for what it is and not to pretend

1:21:19 that it's going to be something much

1:21:21 more involved or glamorous than than

1:21:24 what it is. It's a very, very unique.

1:21:26 I've skated a lot of places and there's

1:21:28 nothing like skating on a Friday night

1:21:31 under the stars or the moon rising over

1:21:33 the city of Boston in the east and it's

1:21:35 just magic. Um, so I would be getting

1:21:39 more and more into it on the committee

1:21:40 and finding out what other people felt

1:21:42 and try to be understanding of other

1:21:44 people's points of view and then add my

1:21:46 own to it based on what I heard and

1:21:48 learned.

1:21:54 the image that you gave of uh of under

1:22:58 there. I owned a house in Martha's

1:23:02 renovations over the years. I was

1:23:04 perhaps miscast as a lawyer. I like to

1:28:00 specific location they can actually use

1:28:03 public transportation. Initially as a a

1:28:07 resident next to Lars Anderson Park, I

1:28:09 was really concerned when initially they

1:28:12 were talking about putting a highest

1:28:14 rank all season used because where I

1:28:17 live, I live on 147 Newton Street. That

1:28:21 intersection a lot of traffic and I was

1:28:24 looking I was really concerned with

1:28:26 that. And when they had the first

1:28:28 meeting about the the rank what to do

1:28:31 with it and I spoke out at the first

1:28:33 meeting one of the suggestion is make it

1:28:37 accessible through public

1:28:38 transportations

1:28:40 and I think that that's where the town

1:28:42 is uh is leading to toward the vision

1:28:45 zero net zero

1:28:48 toward that if you reduce the traffic

1:28:51 you have public transportation available

1:28:54 you can reduce the traffic in increase

1:28:56 the use of that facility and

1:29:02 in I live in precinct 15 there's two

1:29:05 major there's the golf course and

1:29:07 there's laws in this in park and the ice

1:29:09 rink is one of the most usable facility

1:29:13 in the town especially by young kids and

1:29:18 I have a family that usually visit they

1:29:22 visit us they usually come in in the

1:29:25 winter time from Canada. When when they

1:29:28 come to the to the winter time in the

1:29:30 can from Canada, I usually take them to

1:29:32 uh to the ice rink to skate with my my

1:29:35 my my daughter and my son. So, it's

1:29:38 beautiful, especially when the when the

1:29:41 time the sunset, you can see the sunset

1:29:44 glowing on the park on the ice. It's a

1:29:46 beautiful place to be in the winter

1:29:49 time. So I'm really connected to that

1:29:53 project and I would like to to be part

1:29:56 of the review community,

1:29:58 the design review community so that I

1:30:00 can shape it so that it can sit where it

1:30:03 is and it it is accessible, usable and

1:30:07 it's connected to the community. Thank

1:30:10 you.

1:30:11 Uh, now you already mentioned this

1:30:14 somewhat in your opening remarks, but

1:30:16 how does your current experience on the

1:30:18 zoning board of appeals help inform how

1:30:21 you would act as a member on this

1:30:24 committee? Is there some specific

1:30:25 experience that you think will be

1:30:27 particularly useful?

1:30:29 Yes. Uh I have been on the zoning board

1:30:31 of appeal for approximately

1:30:34 uh four years right now and I have seen

1:30:37 a lot of uh uh projects that come in

1:30:40 before the ZBA board and one of uh the

1:30:44 whenever I seen a project I usually see

1:30:46 is it fit and within the within the

1:30:50 community as well is it meeting the the

1:30:52 larger community goals. So you have to

1:30:55 come in with a balanced approach to it

1:30:58 statics massing you have to wait with

1:31:01 the massing whether that project it's

1:31:03 fit in the massing and then you have to

1:31:06 look at the set the static the access

1:31:08 accessibility of the project you have to

1:31:12 look at all take the all those I I

1:31:14 usually see review it in a holistic

1:31:17 approach so I see there's a need for a

1:31:21 certain project okay it's reflect

1:31:24 modernity modernity and as well as I

1:31:27 have to preserve the character of the

1:31:30 neighborhood. So I have to balance both

1:31:32 and at the end I try to come in with a

1:31:35 consensus that achieve the goal the

1:31:39 larger goal and as well as preserving

1:31:42 the character of the neighborhood and

1:31:44 that will be the character of LZ Under

1:31:47 Park and then with the larger community.

1:31:50 Thank you.

1:31:50 Thank you. Anyone else with questions?

1:31:53 All right, seeing none, thank you for

1:31:55 the interview. Uh, we I just want to

1:31:58 provide some broader context for what's

1:32:00 taking place here. So, the select

1:32:02 board's taking an important step forward

1:32:04 in the replacement and upgrade of the

1:32:07 outdoor ice skating rink at Lars

1:32:09 Anderson Park and the supporting

1:32:11 infrastructure around it. This project

1:32:14 also includes the stabilization and

1:32:16 historic restoration of the original

1:32:19 Anderson era elements within the project

1:32:21 area which are an important part of the

1:32:24 park's history and character. This is an

1:32:27 exciting and long-awaited project for

1:32:29 the town. It reflects years of work by

1:32:31 the park and recreation commission, town

1:32:33 staff, community members, and many

1:32:35 others who have helped evaluate the

1:32:37 future of the rink. Uh, by way of brief

1:32:40 background, in 2020, after the

1:32:43 refrigeration system at the Lars

1:32:45 Anderson Park ice rink failed, the Park

1:32:47 and Recreation Commission established an

1:32:49 ice rink task force to evaluate future

1:32:52 options for the facility. A preliminary

1:32:55 feasibility study was conducted in 2022

1:32:58 and the ice rink programming study was

1:33:00 completed in 2024.

1:33:03 Based on those findings, the park and

1:33:05 recreation commission voted to advance a

1:33:08 single open air rink and the select

1:33:11 board supported locating the replacement

1:33:14 rink at the existing site. In May 2025,

1:33:19 town meeting approved community

1:33:20 preservation act funding to advance

1:33:22 design work, public engagement, and the

1:33:25 preparation of construction documents

1:33:27 for the rink on the existing site.

1:33:31 This project is unique because it brings

1:33:33 together recreation, public buildings,

1:33:35 landscape, historic preservation, park

1:33:38 planning, and community use. For that

1:33:40 reason, the design review committee has

1:33:42 been structured to reflect a broad and

1:33:45 collaborative process. The design review

1:33:48 committee will include representatives

1:33:49 from the building commission and the

1:33:51 park and recreation commission who will

1:33:54 serve as chairs along with the select

1:33:55 board representative and uh members of

1:33:58 the preservation commission and planning

1:33:59 board. It will also include key

1:34:01 stakeholders including friends of Lars

1:34:03 Anderson Park youth hockey and two

1:34:06 members of the public and we just heard

1:34:08 two members of the public interview for

1:34:11 those spots. The role of the design

1:34:13 review committee will be to guide the

1:34:15 concept design phase, and help ensure

1:34:17 that the project meets community needs,

1:34:20 respects the site's historic character,

1:34:22 and aligns with the town's broader

1:34:24 goals. This phase will also allow the

1:34:26 town to refine project costs and better

1:34:29 understand potential construction

1:34:31 funding and grant opportunities. The

1:34:35 design review committee will serve in an

1:34:37 advisory role. It will make concept

1:34:39 design recommendations to the building

1:34:40 commission, park and recreation

1:34:42 commission, preservation commission, and

1:34:45 planning board. The design will

1:34:47 ultimately be reviewed and approved by

1:34:49 the select board. Once the concept

1:34:52 design is approved, the consultant team

1:34:54 will proceed with detailed design,

1:34:56 preparation of construction documents,

1:34:58 bidding, and necessary permitting. There

1:35:01 will be several public meetings as a

1:35:03 part of this process. And whether you

1:35:05 are a member of this committee or not,

1:35:07 all are of course welcome to attend and

1:35:10 opine.

1:35:12 Uh these meetings are scheduled for May

1:35:15 18th,

1:35:16 June 22nd, and July 30th. Additional

1:35:20 public meetings will be announced as

1:35:22 needed. These meetings will give

1:35:23 residents, user groups, neighbors, and

1:35:25 other stakeholders opportunities to

1:35:27 learn about the project, provide

1:35:29 feedback, and help shape the design as

1:35:31 it develops. The goal is to move through

1:35:33 a thoughtful, transparent concept design

1:35:36 process that produces a clear, buildable

1:35:39 plan, one that modernizes the rink,

1:35:42 respects the historic Anderson elements,

1:35:44 and improves the surrounding landscape

1:35:47 and infrastructure.

1:35:49 Uh now I do have some procedural

1:35:51 questions uh from town council regarding

1:35:56 what specifically is needed of us as a

1:35:58 board tonight because I see that the

1:36:01 first meeting is scheduled for May 18th.

1:36:04 Uh and we don't have another select

1:36:06 board meeting before May 18th. Typically

1:36:10 we would have

1:36:12 another meeting before we would actually

1:50:07 through it um and you know uh Michael

1:50:10 sent some comments today as well um

1:50:12 which I think are worth reviewing and

1:50:14 discussing. Um a vote would be be more

1:50:17 appropriate at the next meeting. Um but

1:50:19 we wanted to update you on the progress

1:50:21 and talk a little bit about all the work

1:50:23 that's gone into this and where we stand

1:50:25 now and any additional thoughts um that

1:50:29 you all have as we uh continue to work

1:50:31 on this. So this is the uh 2030 roadmap

1:50:35 uh presentation and um just a reminder

1:50:39 um the goal of this is to develop is to

1:50:43 develop a means for the select board and

1:50:46 staff to be proactive in not just

1:50:50 reactive in determining what our

1:50:51 mid-range goals are as a community. You

1:50:54 all set the agenda. you set the goals

1:50:57 and objectives and we on staff are

1:50:59 tasked with implementing those goals and

1:51:01 objectives um and figuring out what the

1:51:04 measurements of success are to report

1:51:06 back to you. So we want to capture those

1:51:08 shared priority areas of yours in this

1:51:11 document and guide us uh you know all

1:51:15 all both members of staff but also the

1:51:18 select board itself as it continues to

1:51:20 have policy discussions uh in their

1:51:22 work. um and to do that through changing

1:51:25 environments, which can be tough. Um but

1:51:28 we want to come up with guiding

1:51:29 principles and metrics that we can use

1:51:31 to measure those guiding principles so

1:51:33 that um there's the opportunity um for

1:51:37 staff to focus on longer range projects

1:51:40 um and uh be confident that they are in

1:51:44 alignment with the community's

1:51:45 priorities. Um, I think a lot of this

1:51:48 stemmed out of these, you know,

1:51:50 conversations that were had about

1:51:52 projects that were longer range. You

1:51:54 know, folks coming in, you know, at like

1:51:56 with a project that was like an 18month

1:51:58 to two-year horizon coming to the select

1:52:00 board, you know, maybe a year into it

1:52:02 and having the select board's priorities

1:52:05 have changed in that period of time. Um

1:52:08 again there's no fault of the board for

1:52:10 that but trying to figure out where the

1:52:12 kind of loadar um uh priorities are so

1:52:16 that we can follow those uh and not run

1:52:19 a foul of your what what you want from

1:52:21 us um on those longer range projects is

1:52:23 really an aim of this process.

1:52:27 So um we hired Abby to facilitate the

1:52:30 development and Abby has been uh is now

1:52:33 more than familiar with the Brookline

1:52:35 process of doing business. uh and has

1:52:37 really um just been a remarkable uh

1:52:40 partner in this. We want to thank her

1:52:42 and CBI for all the work they've done.

1:52:44 Um it started with brainstorming and

1:52:46 idea generation um way back in the fall,

1:52:49 interviews with the select board, the

1:52:50 select board office and department

1:52:51 heads. A review of all of our existing

1:52:54 goals and plans. Some departments have

1:52:56 very detailed goals and objectives. DPW

1:52:58 is is an example. Planning is an

1:53:00 example. Some do not. Uh and that's

1:53:02 okay. it's it's just the you know some

1:53:04 of them the processes that they follow.

1:53:06 Um we tried to use to that to develop

1:53:09 values goals and strategies um through

1:53:12 workshops iterative review with the

1:53:14 select board and the select board office

1:53:16 and department heads thinking about how

1:53:17 to prioritize things. In the past when

1:53:19 we've talked about this folks have said

1:53:21 everything is a priority which means you

1:53:23 know nothing is a priority. Uh and so we

1:53:25 try very hard to figure out what needs

1:53:27 to be done now versus what needs to be

1:53:29 done in the long term. And you can see

1:53:31 that in the uh the uh roadmap tracker

1:53:34 talking about what needs to be done

1:53:35 when. Um and then decisions on

1:53:38 implementation and update processes.

1:53:40 Who's responsible for the things that

1:53:41 you put into the road map and what are

1:53:44 the metrics that we are going to use for

1:53:45 to measure that success and when are we

1:53:47 going to update you about them?

1:53:49 So here are some of the key elements.

1:53:51 You have to have values to inform goals

1:53:53 and decision- making. What do we believe

1:53:55 in? We have to have actual goals. You

1:53:57 need to think about strategies to

1:53:59 operationalize those goals. Then we need

1:54:00 to actually come up with actions to meet

1:54:02 those strategies. Um actions are the

1:54:04 most likely thing to change over the

1:54:06 five-year timeline and those are the

1:54:07 things that are actually assigned out.

1:54:08 Okay, department A, you're going to

1:54:10 handle this. Select board as our elected

1:54:12 representatives, you're going to handle

1:54:13 this. Um but those things can change. Um

1:54:16 and that's okay. Um that's why we have

1:54:19 these regular touch points for feedback

1:54:20 and thinking about how best to implement

1:54:23 them. But what we as you get higher up

1:54:25 in that hierarchy, we want to try and

1:54:26 think of ways, you know, things that are

1:54:29 foundational, things that can um can be

1:54:34 um

1:54:36 can continue to guide um the town

1:54:38 regardless of kind of where, you know,

1:54:42 where things wind up um in terms of how

1:54:44 votes are taken and so forth. That's not

1:54:46 to say that priorities can't change.

1:54:47 Priorities can change and they should

1:54:48 change in response to the community, the

1:54:51 community's voice. But we want to think

1:54:52 of that in terms of practical things

1:54:55 like how do we get the work done? How do

1:54:57 we get the basic work done of running a

1:54:58 community? Um what are those sort of

1:55:01 loadar uh guide uh guiding uh lights

1:55:05 that we need to follow? And then you

1:55:07 have these key performance indicators.

1:55:08 What do we you know how do we measure

1:55:09 success? KPIs

1:55:11 very it's consultant lingo. Uh

1:55:15 so this is the expanded sort of uh

1:55:17 framework here. Um you can see here this

1:55:20 is a sample of what the document looks

1:55:22 like. Um here's a goal. Here are the

1:55:25 strategies. Um and here are the actions

1:55:27 and you can see where they're assigned

1:55:29 and where in the timeline they fall. Um

1:55:32 so you have for example this this goal

1:55:34 of develop uh deliver core services

1:55:36 reliably and efficiently within the

1:55:38 limits of the available resources.

1:55:40 Pretty broad uh pretty agreeable. Um but

1:55:43 okay, how do we do that? One of the

1:55:44 strategies is to document and

1:55:46 communicate to residents the strength of

1:55:47 town services and improve transparency

1:55:49 on our performance. Do people know what

1:55:51 it is that we're doing? Do people know

1:55:53 that uh how how those things are going

1:55:55 for them? Uh can they see where things

1:55:57 are good and where things need

1:55:58 improvement? So you can see um the uh uh

1:56:02 then the actual actions down here um are

1:56:05 for example document the required base

1:56:06 services that each department provides

1:56:09 um the level of service, the enforcement

1:56:10 requirements and so forth. And then you

1:56:12 see the KPIs down there. Um the KPIs are

1:56:15 department set service goals by by the

1:56:18 end of FY27, a dashboard developed to

1:56:20 track service provision by the end of

1:56:22 FY29, and then the reporting dashboard

1:56:24 is published at least annually. So those

1:56:26 are your KPIs and the targets there.

1:56:29 So the values of Brookline are being

1:56:32 were in the current draft are being a

1:56:34 welcoming, accessible, accepting and

1:56:36 diverse community, being a predictable

1:56:38 and stable place for residents,

1:56:39 visitors, and businesses. And the values

1:56:41 of government are these continuous

1:56:43 improvement of efficiency and outcomes,

1:56:45 transparency and responsiveness,

1:56:47 decision-making that incorporates

1:56:48 feedback from affecting stakeholders,

1:56:50 broader community based on the best

1:56:52 information available and conducted in a

1:56:54 fiscally responsible manner, good

1:56:56 governance, collaboration across

1:56:57 departments and public bodies,

1:56:59 commitment to the values of diversity,

1:57:00 equity, and inclusion for all and

1:57:02 excellence in service provision and a

1:57:04 growing economy.

1:57:06 Here are the seven core goals. Deliver

1:57:09 core services to residents as we talked

1:57:11 about earlier reliably and efficiently

1:57:13 with the resources we have. Strengthen

1:57:15 the alignment of goals and practices.

1:57:17 Improve efficiency and strengthen

1:57:19 governance. Increase our fiscal health

1:57:20 and stability. Improve communication and

1:57:22 engagement. Increase the racial and

1:57:24 economic diversity of Brookline.

1:57:26 Maintain and improve the quality of our

1:57:28 infrastructure and improve the town's

1:57:30 ability to mitigate climate change and

1:57:32 increase the resilience of our assets to

1:57:34 climate change.

1:57:37 So here are the how we talk about

1:57:38 tracking progress and updating the 2030

1:57:40 roadmap. Um the select board office um

1:57:43 our office will track progress on the

1:57:45 KPIs and the status of each action

1:57:47 annually in the first quarter of the

1:57:49 year. Um the select the first quarter of

1:57:52 the uh of the year we will update and

1:57:54 review the road map annually at the

1:57:58 you have these quarterly workshops

1:58:00 that's the first one of the year of the

1:58:02 calendar year. uh and then the updated

1:58:06 of the fiscal year goals at the summer

2:00:13 to try. Um, that said, it seems very

2:01:52 when it says maintain and improve

2:02:23 sidewalk uh sidewalk and concrete laying

2:02:26 and and blacktop going down is is due to

2:02:31 like to see those filled. Um and then

2:02:33 and we could develop strategies on how

2:02:40 revenue study committee um highlighted

2:02:46 related to green energy. One of which I

2:02:57 change. Uh in the expansion revenue

2:03:04 charge. Um, and I think that that is

2:03:08 into. Um, I think that we'll be able to

2:03:11 handle peaks and valleys of electricity

2:03:14 demand. And so I think that that belongs

2:03:16 in that section, too. But I'll I'll stop

2:03:17 there.

2:03:19 Thank you.

2:03:20 Um,

2:04:38 uh in

2:11:37 example. There's like a number of you

2:11:39 know plans you should consult and

2:11:41 whatnot. So we could remove that. And

2:11:43 then the second question is should you

2:11:45 have your own legislative agenda of what

2:11:46 you want to see happen each year and

2:11:48 that I think is still on the table.

2:11:50 Chaz.

2:11:50 Yeah. I would just say that that and

2:11:51 that mirrors some you know what goes on

2:11:53 at the state level. Right. The governor

2:11:54 has an agenda requires the legislator

2:11:57 legislature to say yes. Um and so the

2:11:59 governor will present an agenda to the

2:12:01 legislature. the legislature will look

2:12:02 at it, poke holes in it, move it around,

2:12:05 modify it, pass it as they see fit, and

2:12:07 then the governor has to say, "Well, I

2:12:08 either agree I agree with this or veto

2:12:10 it." Except you don't even have a veto.

2:12:11 So, uh, there you go. Um, but the the

2:12:15 the thing that I think is important, I

2:12:17 agree we that surveying maybe not the

2:12:20 right term, but one of the things that I

2:12:22 think is actually done we've done better

2:12:24 over the last couple years is be more

2:12:27 connected to the leadership of the

2:12:29 advisory committee. And I think that

2:12:31 really has made a difference in terms of

2:12:33 building a better you know functioning

2:12:36 town government um when it comes to um

2:12:40 being in alignment early and I think you

2:12:42 know examples where there have been

2:12:43 hiccups in that have been where either

2:12:45 through timing issues or what have you

2:12:48 we haven't been on the same page because

2:12:50 they and then they feel like oh we

2:12:52 didn't get any notice about this and

2:12:53 that's you know they're the finance

2:12:55 committee um and town meeting and you

2:12:57 know town meeting relies on that and so

2:12:59 I do think it is important to keep them

2:13:01 engaged in the process especially if

2:13:03 there are things coming up that are of

2:13:05 major importance to you the select board

2:13:08 that we can continue to have those kind

2:13:09 of conversations early and often you can

2:13:12 say look this is really a priority of

2:13:13 the board you know this is you know or

2:13:16 for example you know when we were

2:13:18 dealing with you know tough collective

2:13:19 bargaining issues right we talked about

2:13:21 the fire department and minimum staffing

2:13:22 like we had a meeting you know I I made

2:13:24 sure that the leadership of the advisory

2:13:26 committee knew hey this is coming down

2:13:28 the pike it's going to be a big and you

2:13:30 should, you know, I'm not telling you

2:13:32 how to feel about it, but I'm telling

2:13:33 you this is an issue that's coming up

2:13:35 and will need to be discussed. And so,

2:13:37 um, having that connection is really

2:13:39 important. Um, and I think actually

2:13:41 builds goodwill and and and make sure

2:13:44 that we are, um, better aligned, uh,

2:13:47 within the obviously, you know, the

2:13:49 separation of powers, uh, and the

2:13:52 ability of the legislative branch to

2:13:54 ultimately pass law.

2:13:56 A different verb here I think is

2:13:59 warranted.

2:14:00 I I like that idea of different verb

2:14:02 because I also agree that the executive

2:14:04 our our the select board here should

2:14:07 have a legislative agenda and it should

2:14:09 be pronounced early in the process um of

2:14:11 what we want to see so that the advisory

2:14:13 committee has time to digest the

2:14:15 executive boards kind of what they're

2:14:18 picturing um and then make changes and

2:14:20 adjust through the legislative process

2:14:21 as it needs to. But I I I agree with a

2:14:23 lot of what you said, Chaz, like that um

2:14:25 by having a legislative agenda, we're

2:14:27 giving the legislature a lot of time to

2:14:30 react to what the select board may do in

2:14:32 the future, which I think is really

2:14:33 beneficial for the working relationship

2:14:35 between the advisory committee and the

2:14:36 select board.

2:14:38 So I I agree with Michael's suggestion

2:14:41 on this point mostly. The the one caveat

2:14:44 is I do think having check-ins with town

2:14:46 meeting can be important because there's

2:14:49 a tendency well-intentioned but there's

2:14:51 a tendency for there to be last minute

2:14:53 amendments sometimes a town meeting and

2:14:56 if town meeting members are apprised of

2:14:58 what we're working on and what the

2:15:00 legislative agenda is then that can be

2:15:03 helpful because then town meeting

2:15:04 members don't feel ambushed and

2:15:07 obviously they do have opportunities to

2:15:09 come to select board meetings to

2:15:10 participate in board and commission

2:15:12 meetings But still, it doesn't hurt to

2:15:14 make sure that they are given some of

2:15:17 these big picture updates from time to

2:15:19 time. So, I agree with Michael that

2:15:21 surveying is not really appropriate, but

2:15:23 I think check-ins can be helpful.

2:15:26 Uh, and then when we're done with this

2:15:29 subtopic,

2:15:30 uh, I want to address, uh, Paul Ser's

2:15:34 suggestions from public comment. I know

2:15:36 you brought this up as well, Amanda. Uh

2:15:38 I think they make a lot of sense for us

2:15:40 to incorporate his uh suggested edit

2:15:45 which would add to that sentence we have

2:15:47 in goal in goal three and include

2:15:49 seeking out mixed use including

2:15:51 commercial in residential opportunities.

2:19:44 Give it a second. Great. Uh, thank you

2:19:47 all for having me here tonight. For

2:19:49 those of you who don't yet know me, my

2:19:51 name is Jake Collins and I am the

2:19:54 community planner in the planning

2:19:56 department's housing and community

2:19:58 development division. I am here tonight

2:20:01 to discuss the fiscal year 27 federal

2:20:04 year fiscal federal fiscal year 26 HUD

2:20:08 annual action plan the town of Brooklyn

2:20:11 primarily focuses on our CDBG work

2:20:13 although we do get home funds. So, as

2:20:16 some of you may recall, my colleague

2:20:18 David Guzman brought the draft annual

2:20:20 action plan to the select board last

2:20:23 month, and now we are here requesting um

2:20:27 that you approve the submission of the

2:20:29 plan to HUD, as well as a few associated

2:20:33 actions in advance of our May 15th

2:20:36 deadline.

2:20:38 So, I won't read through the annual

2:20:40 action plan objectives. Um, but I will

2:20:44 point out that our available funding has

2:20:46 increased $17,000 this year, which is

2:20:50 actually a big success. Um, generally we

2:20:53 see decrease in funding, even a moderate

2:20:56 increase like 17,000 allows us to better

2:20:58 support our sub recipients.

2:21:02 Um, for those of you who have not been

2:21:04 along with us on the ride through this

2:21:06 CDBG cycle, um, here's a timeline. Um,

2:21:10 we had a request for proposal in

2:21:12 January, opened in January of last year.

2:21:14 The CDBG advisory committee then

2:21:17 deliberated on the responses

2:21:21 and um, developed allocations.

2:21:25 We drafted the annual action plan. It

2:21:27 requires 30-day public review.

2:21:30 uh we published it widely and we did not

2:21:32 receive any com uh comments and so here

2:21:36 tonight we are really just looking for

2:21:38 final approval and a vote from you

2:21:40 before submitting it.

2:21:43 As a reminder, we've kind of identified

2:21:45 four um priority areas in the action

2:21:48 plan. Um this year we have been able to

2:21:51 allocate money under the affordable

2:21:52 housing category as well as public

2:21:54 services.

2:21:59 And here is the updated uh recommended

2:22:02 allocation chart um which reflects the

2:22:05 $17,000

2:22:07 increase. Um we used the or we asked for

2:22:11 guidance from the CDBG advisory

2:22:13 committee on how best to allocate this

2:22:15 additional funding. Um what really

2:22:17 happened was that all eligible public

2:22:19 services got approximately $880 uh

2:22:23 dollars um added to their total

2:22:25 allocation

2:22:27 um when they were applicable and that's

2:22:29 basically to maintain the 15% cap. Uh

2:22:32 the rest of the funds the remaining

2:22:34 funds went to BHA for their capital

2:22:37 projects at the high street at their

2:22:39 high street veterans development. So,

2:22:42 they received roughly 15,000. Um,

2:22:45 otherwise, everything else remains

2:22:47 unchanged.

2:22:50 And here's just some language for the

2:22:52 vote. I'm happy to answer any questions

2:22:58 at this time. Thank you. All right. Any

2:23:02 comments from anyone?

2:42:35 contract plus two two-year renewal

2:42:43 Share System. Do we have anyone who will
