2:42:48 Hey, good evening. Uh, can you guys hear

2:42:50 me? Okay.

2:42:51 Yes.

2:42:53 Uh, I'll reiterate what other people

2:42:55 have said. Congratulations to Amanda and

2:42:56 Anthony and cong congratulations to

2:42:58 chairperson Pearlman and thank you to

2:43:01 Bernard Green. Uh, it's been a pleasure.

2:43:03 Um, my name is Sam DS. I am the deputy

2:43:06 director of engineering for the town.

2:43:09 I've been with the town now going on

2:43:11 four years and I've been very involved

2:43:12 with the blue bikes um,

2:43:16 system uh, as a proud owner and original

2:43:19 member. Um, Brookline was one of the

2:43:22 four original municipalities who started

2:43:24 the bike share system uh back in 2012

2:43:27 with Cambridge, Somerville, uh, and

2:43:29 Boston. Um, and tonight I am here. Um,

2:43:33 I'm happy to give the board a quick

2:43:35 overview of Blue Bikes. Uh, before we,

2:43:38 uh, dive into the contract request,

2:43:41 uh, I will just share my screen

2:43:44 briefly.

2:43:49 So, for those of you who do not know

2:43:51 about uh Blue Bikes, uh we currently

2:43:53 just hit uh our 600th station across the

2:43:57 13 municipalities that uh own uh and

2:44:01 maintain the network. Um we have

2:44:05 expanded into Watertown,

2:44:07 uh as far north as Salem, uh Newton, uh

2:44:11 Quinsey is about to to join, uh the

2:44:14 network in the next year. Um, and so

2:44:17 this is a system that is owned by

2:44:19 Brooklyn and the other municipalities.

2:44:21 Um, we are, um,

2:44:25 uh, we are purchasing the stations and

2:44:27 the bikes and we are asking Lyft to

2:44:29 manage the day-to-day operations of this

2:44:32 system that currently, um, sees over 4

2:44:35 million rides taken per year. Uh, since

2:44:39 2020, we have registered over 22 million

2:44:42 rides across uh, the network. Uh so the

2:44:45 blue bike system really plays an

2:44:47 integral role in in transportation

2:44:50 uh for the for the greater Boston uh

2:44:53 area. Uh it's the reason why so many

2:44:56 different uh grant programs from the

2:44:58 state level and as high as the the

2:45:01 federal uh federal level uh fund the

2:45:04 expansion of this network because it

2:45:06 offers 247

2:45:09 um access to to transportation.

2:45:13 Uh we have been uh working on an

2:45:15 expansion plan uh that went through

2:45:17 robust public process and the

2:45:19 transportation board recently uh and we

2:45:21 partnered closely with uh Boston

2:45:23 University uh around that planning. Uh

2:45:26 I'm not here to talk about that tonight.

2:45:28 Um uh as I mentioned there are many

2:45:30 grant programs that are are funding the

2:45:33 current expansion of this. Uh we are

2:45:35 very fortunate to have received uh a

2:45:38 legislative earmark from representative

2:45:39 Vlo as well as a number of mass DOT uh

2:45:43 grants and as I mentioned an upcoming uh

2:45:46 federal highway grant through their

2:45:48 congestion relief program uh that

2:45:50 awarded the the greater Boston um area

2:45:55 um I believe it was $20 million um in

2:45:59 funding. Um so the um the last uh last

2:46:06 contract with Lyft which previously was

2:46:08 known as Motivate

2:46:10 um started in 2017 and ran a similar

2:46:13 course of a 5-year contract with two

2:46:15 two-year renewals. Uh we went through a

2:46:18 very uh robust RFP process uh and a

2:46:21 blind evaluation

2:46:23 um for um for proposals to operate the

2:46:27 blue bikes network. Uh this includes

2:46:29 maintaining the bicycles, maintaining

2:46:31 the stations, uh operating customer

2:46:34 service, um and it's a it's a it's a

2:46:37 large undertaking. And so uh through

2:46:40 this RFP process, uh the 13

2:46:43 municipalities work together, uh and we

2:46:45 have uh decided to continue with Lyft

2:46:48 for uh the additional uh fiveyear plus

2:46:51 two two-year renewal options. Uh and the

2:46:54 contract that I'm asking for the select

2:46:56 board to enter into tonight uh was

2:46:59 originally agreed to by the city of

2:47:01 Boston and subsequently has been also

2:47:03 signed by the city of Somerville uh and

2:47:05 the city of Cambridge. Uh the other

2:47:07 municipalities are still working to

2:47:09 finalize their contracts. Uh but uh we

2:47:12 are the core four uh municipalities and

2:47:16 um I'd be happy to answer any questions

2:47:20 um that you all have about this uh uh

2:47:22 opportunity to renew the contract with

2:47:24 Lyft to continue to maintain and operate

2:47:28 uh the town's uh portion of the blue

2:47:31 bikes broader network. Thank you.

2:47:35 Any questions?

2:47:37 Michael,

2:47:38 um I'm wondering if you can share the

2:47:42 amount of of revenue from this program

2:47:45 that goes to the town. Um and what

2:47:48 percentage of that revenue flows back to

2:47:51 the operators uh as part of this

2:47:53 contract?

2:47:56 I will do my best to to answer that. So

2:47:58 um each year um it of course our revenue

2:48:01 uh is continuing to grow as writership

2:48:04 uh numbers grow as well. Uh the town

2:48:07 sees approximately 15 to $20,000

2:48:10 uh in revenue uh every year uh that goes

2:48:14 back into an operating fund uh that is

2:48:17 held by Lyft. Uh this is to cover

2:48:19 expenses, unexpected expenses that might

2:48:21 come up if a station breaks. Uh we're

2:48:24 able to use this um that revenue uh to

2:48:27 fix, you know, fix the system uh replace

2:48:30 bikes if they break. Um that revenue uh

2:48:34 is also only 15 to $20,000 because we

2:48:37 currently are uh subsidizing

2:48:40 uh uh we are currently in a a model that

2:48:44 subsidize subsidizes rides for both

2:48:47 income eligible members um town

2:48:49 employees uh and also uh the new ebikes

2:48:53 that have been introduced or the e

2:48:54 assist bikes. Um so all the 13

2:48:58 municipalities have agreed to subsidize

2:49:01 um a certain percentage of uh cost to

2:49:05 help help maintain a low level a low

2:49:08 enough level that it entices wrership uh

2:49:11 but also not at the expense of not

2:49:12 generating any revenue. Um so I hope

2:49:16 that somewhat answers the revenue

2:49:18 question. Uh and then in terms of the

2:49:21 the annual costs that go to Lyft, I will

2:49:25 have to cycle back or circle back with

2:49:27 you on the exact dollar value that Lyft

2:49:30 gets from operating uh the system. Uh

2:49:33 but I know that it's been agreed to by

2:49:36 um our fellow municipalities.

2:49:39 Thank you.

2:49:41 Of course.

2:49:42 Anyone else with questions or comments?

2:49:47 All right. Seeing none, I move that we

2:49:51 approve the 5-year contract with Lift

2:49:53 Bikes and Scooters LLC to operate the

2:49:56 regional Blue Bikes Bike Share System.

2:49:59 Bernard,

2:49:59 yes.

2:50:00 Michael,

2:50:01 yes.

2:50:01 Amanda,

2:50:02 yes.

2:50:03 Anthony,

2:50:04 yes.

2:50:04 And I also vote yes.

2:50:07 Thank you very much. I appreciate your

2:50:09 time.

2:50:10 And with that, we will now move to item

2:50:13 4.

2:50:17 which is the question of approving

2:50:19 increases in various fines and fees

2:50:21 including parking fines as recommended

2:50:24 by the transportation board.

2:50:26 Sure. So we have two sets of proposed

2:50:28 fine increase uh fine and fee increases

2:50:30 for you. The first is around parking

2:50:32 fines and the second is around building

2:50:34 permit fees as well as the fees for uh

2:50:36 certificates of occupancy. Um the um

2:50:40 motor vehicle parking fines is uh one

2:50:43 that came before you. um uh a while ago

2:50:47 um in back in April. Um this was

2:50:50 envisioned as part of the uh budget for

2:50:52 FY27. This increase in fines and fees as

2:50:55 a means of offsetting any potential

2:50:57 override that would be put to the

2:50:58 voters. The single largest source of

2:51:01 potential increase here is in parking

2:51:03 fines. Our parking fines have not been

2:51:05 raised since 2007. um they are in most

2:51:09 cases uh between $25 and $30 for various

2:51:13 violations. Other communities including

2:51:15 Boston have raised them to 40 and

2:51:16 beyond. The question about a fine is

2:51:19 unlike a fee which is supposed to be

2:51:21 assessed only for to provide the cost of

2:51:23 the service, the fine is about deterring

2:51:25 behavior. So it's a question of um is it

2:51:28 is the fine commensurate with the

2:51:30 behavior you're intending to deter. Um

2:51:32 ultimately, um and then the other aspect

2:51:35 of this is that the state caps the

2:51:37 amount of any fine um in Brooklyn that

2:51:41 is basically not an initial fine is not

2:51:43 to exceed $50. Um if it's paid uh beyond

2:51:48 21 days of the date of the ticket um but

2:51:50 before it gets reported to the motor

2:51:52 vehicle registar then that increases to

2:51:54 $55 and then after that once that report

2:51:57 is in we can't charge more than $70. So

2:52:00 those are the caps for the fees and f uh

2:52:02 for the fines. So with that in mind, um

2:52:04 in your packet is a memo uh reflecting

2:52:07 all fines that are not currently at $40.

2:52:11 Um and we are recommending that you

2:52:13 increase those fines to $40 in all

2:52:16 instances except for parking on a

2:52:18 sidewalk or parking in a crosswalk.

2:52:20 there. The transportation board

2:52:22 recommended and we on staff agreed um

2:52:24 that given the severity of that and the

2:52:26 desire to deter that behavior that an

2:52:28 increase to $50 would be more

2:52:30 appropriate under those circumstances.

2:52:32 So that would be the maximum potential

2:52:33 fine that we could levy. Um the

2:52:35 transportation board, this is, you know,

2:52:38 transportation board is also a creature

2:52:39 of special act, a special statute. Um it

2:52:43 has certain roles and responsibilities

2:52:45 that it plays, but there's also a role

2:52:46 for the select board to play. Last time

2:52:48 we raised fines, we asked the T-board to

2:52:52 make a recommendation to the select

2:52:53 board and the select board to ultimately

2:52:55 vote on it. And that was the process we

2:52:56 followed here. The T-board has

2:52:58 recommended unanimously uh that the fee

2:53:00 that the fine schedule in your packet be

2:53:02 adopted. Um and um under these

2:53:06 circumstances um we are going to take

2:53:08 some of the money that we anticipate

2:53:09 raising from these increased fines and

2:53:11 reinvest it directly in parking control.

2:53:15 um you know the work that needs to be

2:53:17 done to ensure that we are doing better

2:53:19 at um collecting uh collecting fees

2:53:23 appropriately. Um uh assessing fines

2:53:26 appropriately um not uh losing out uh

2:53:30 when someone appeals successfully

2:53:32 appeals a ticket because a meter is

2:53:34 broken etc. Trying to get a trying to

2:53:36 build a better process around ensuring

2:53:38 that our parking structure is

2:53:41 appropriately managed and maintained.

2:53:43 Um, and that includes looking at

2:53:45 Passport as a system. Um, there aren't

2:53:47 that many other mobile parking games in

2:53:50 town. It's it's not a racket, but it's a

2:53:52 it's a it's a closed market. Uh, and so

2:53:54 we're trying our best. We But part of

2:53:56 that is assessing whether or not we

2:53:59 re-evaluate that reevaluate that

2:54:00 relationship, talk to competitors, talk

2:54:03 to Park Mobile, which is the other big

2:54:04 game in in the region. Um, and just

2:54:07 really assess the landscape there. So

2:54:09 reinvesting directly in that, but some

2:54:11 of this money will also flow through

2:54:12 general revenue as it has uh for a

2:54:15 while. We anticipate that it will bring

2:54:17 in around a million dollars of

2:54:18 additional revenue. Um most of which

2:54:20 will flow through the town school split.

2:54:23 Okay. All right.

2:54:25 Yeah. Um you mentioned

2:54:30 mentioned problems with the parking

2:54:31 meters. Is some of this money going to

2:54:33 go to maintaining parking meters so that

2:54:36 you people can't appeal based on a

2:54:40 broken parking meter?

2:54:41 So, one of the things that I'm working

2:54:42 on right now, we're we're building cross

2:54:44 departmental working groups um in the

2:54:46 departments to try and figure out these

2:54:48 issues. And so yes, one of the one of

2:54:50 the things that I want to do here is sit

2:54:52 down with police, DPW, treasury and

2:54:54 figure all that out because right now

2:54:57 how parking used to work was basically,

2:54:59 you know, someone who really liked

2:55:01 parking took it on and it was about the

2:55:02 person and not about the process. And

2:55:04 right now there's a vacuum in there. Um

2:55:06 it's not that anyone it's not that

2:55:08 anyone isn't doing their job. It's just

2:55:10 that no one's one job is oh I got I got

2:55:13 to I got to be the parking zar. I got to

2:55:14 deal with this. it was just something

2:55:16 that people a succession of individuals

2:55:18 took on in the town and worked on and

2:55:20 that's obviously not a sustainable

2:55:21 model. So a part of that is building out

2:55:24 processes internally to then ensure

2:55:27 because there is a um there is a line

2:55:29 item in the budget for parking meter

2:55:31 replacement um and it's about making

2:55:33 sure that that money is is that the

2:55:35 right amount of money? Do we need to

2:55:36 increase it? Do we need to change the

2:55:38 replacement schedule? All those are

2:55:40 questions that we want to answer.

2:55:41 Okay, one other question. Um

2:55:45 Um, excuse me. Quarters or parking

2:55:48 meters paid with quarters? A lot of

2:55:52 people really really want to continue um

2:55:56 being able to put quarters in the

2:55:57 meters.

2:55:58 Yeah.

2:55:58 Um, any plans to change that?

2:56:01 No.

2:56:01 Okay, good.

2:56:02 I am I'm one of those people. Um,

2:56:05 I am too.

2:56:05 I don't like it tracking where I park.

2:56:07 Uh,

2:56:09 Anthony.

2:56:10 Um, so I understand that the the new

2:56:12 fine structure um and be capped out at

2:56:14 $50, correct?

2:56:15 Yes. Y Okay. So, I see the majority of

2:56:17 them are brought to 40 except for on

2:56:20 sidewalk and in crosswalk.

2:56:22 Um, I just did quick inflation

2:56:24 calculator. $30 in 2007 is $48 today.

2:56:28 Yeah. So, what that means is we're

2:56:30 saying that the social harm of a parking

2:56:32 limit permit violation is less in 2007

2:56:36 than what it is today with this current

2:56:38 fee structure. I'm not really

2:56:40 comfortable with that.

2:56:41 I think I think the I I think all the

2:56:44 fines should be put to $50 to be

2:56:45 perfectly frank. I mean, one of them is

2:56:47 double parking. I can't think of one of

2:56:49 the more one of the more dangerous

2:56:50 things on here is double parking in

2:56:52 regards to cycling or anything else um

2:56:54 and pedestrians trying to cross. I think

2:56:55 there's a lot on here that could justify

2:56:57 moving up to 50. Um, and I don't think

2:56:59 I'm super comfortable with the current

2:57:01 fee structure, um, given the fact that

2:57:03 the majority of these are under where

2:57:04 inflation would put them in 2007.

2:57:06 And I think so the other big thing here

2:57:08 that we got to keep an eye on, right, is

2:57:10 what where other communities are. That's

2:57:11 a big and so, you know, and we're going

2:57:13 to talk about this when it comes to

2:57:14 building permit fees, which is a

2:57:16 different rubric, um, but it's a similar

2:57:19 thing. What do other communities do?

2:57:21 Right now, Boston does have fines that

2:57:23 go above $40. There's some that are in

2:57:25 the 70s, some in the $90 range, but they

2:57:28 got a special act at the legislature to

2:57:29 do that. Um, right now, our special act,

2:57:32 our own little special act of the

2:57:33 legislature, which we haven't amended,

2:57:36 says that we can't go above 50 and the,

2:57:39 you know, the structure here and we're

2:57:41 following what Cambridge has recently

2:57:43 done, right? Cambridge recently

2:57:44 increased a number of their fines to

2:57:45 $40. So, we feel comfortable saying, you

2:57:48 know, if Cambridge is doing it, we can

2:57:50 do it. Newton, by contrast, is still at

2:57:52 25 for a lot of their fines and fees.

2:57:54 Um, so Somerville is a little higher.

2:57:57 One of the kind of second part of this

2:57:58 and the thing that I talked to the

2:58:00 transportation board about because I I

2:58:01 take your point. Um, it may well be time

2:58:04 for us to submit a request to the

2:58:06 legislature to increase the amount that

2:58:08 we're able to pay to find. Um, you know,

2:58:11 to both do what we're doing now, which

2:58:14 is to increase it commensurate with, you

2:58:16 know, comparable communities, but also

2:58:18 then go to the state and say, "Look,

2:58:20 you've kept us at $50.

2:58:23 Clearly, there are social goals that are

2:58:24 not being met here. And when you adjust

2:58:26 for inflation, by increasing to $40, the

2:58:29 the real dollar value is still lower

2:58:31 than it was um in 2007.

2:58:33 Do do you think that it's worthwhile to

2:58:35 put them all at 50 in the meantime, or

2:58:36 do you think it's it's not worth that?

2:58:39 I think it's a risk. Like, as with

2:58:41 anything, it's a risk. Okay. Right. Um I

2:58:44 I don't think it's I don't think it's

2:58:47 irrational. I wouldn't be like, "Oh, no,

2:58:49 no, don't do that." Um, but I do think

2:58:51 that you're more likely to make it stick

2:58:53 when you have a clear comparator that

2:58:55 you can point to and say, "Hey, that,

2:58:57 you know, we're just doing what

2:58:58 Cambridge did. We're not we're just

2:59:00 doing what Somerville did. We're doing,

2:59:02 you know, we're not where Boston is, but

2:59:04 we're not Boston. We can't get away with

2:59:06 putting some of these things at 70."

2:59:08 So, I feel more comfortable

2:59:10 um at the $40 range, but I'm I wouldn't,

2:59:13 you know, I'm not rejecting it out of

2:59:15 hand. I'm not saying no no like if

2:59:16 that's what the board wants to do,

2:59:18 that's okay. I just think it runs more

2:59:19 of a risk. Um

2:59:21 I'd like to hear what my colleagues

2:59:22 think on that.

2:59:25 Anyone want to be heard on

2:59:26 what what is the risk?

2:59:27 Well, that someone will Well, for one

2:59:29 thing, as as someone pointed out, I

2:59:31 don't think this is the transportation

2:59:33 board. I think it was at I think it was

2:59:34 here. Um someone pointed out people are

2:59:36 more likely to appeal a ticket that is

2:59:38 of a higher dollar value. Um, some

2:59:40 people are likely to, you know, eat the

2:59:42 cost of a $25 ticket, but if they say

2:59:44 the ticket is $40, it's just there's

2:59:47 there's a psychological barrier that may

2:59:48 be crossed there. And I think it might

2:59:50 be more likely to be crossed at 50 than

2:59:51 40. Um, but again, that's just armchair

2:59:54 psychology. I don't have any data on

2:59:56 that. Um, I I would be

3:00:00 and then once you enter into that

3:00:01 challenge process, then you have this

3:00:03 question of is the fee proportional to

3:00:06 the offense? uh is the is the fine sorry

3:00:08 proportional to the offense uh is

3:00:10 proportional to the harm that's being

3:00:11 caused. And what we talked about with

3:00:14 the transportation board is yeah we have

3:00:15 this policy that we really want to

3:00:18 incentivize safety on sidewalks and in

3:00:20 crosswalks. So parking on sidewalks or

3:00:23 in crosswalks is a particularly um

3:00:26 antisocial anti-pedestrian activity that

3:00:28 we want to limit. Um once you start

3:00:31 increasing that across the board, you

3:00:33 know, you're going to have to make the

3:00:34 argument that yes, as a as a ma as this

3:00:37 matter, um you know, we are, you know,

3:00:41 it it's um it's commensurate with the

3:00:43 with the offense in question and

3:00:45 reasonable under the circumstances. The

3:00:47 big argument that you have going for

3:00:48 you, and the reason why I wouldn't say

3:00:49 no to $50, is that the legislature let

3:00:52 you do it. Um the legislature passed a

3:00:54 special act 20 some odd years ago saying

3:00:58 you can bring your fines up to $50 if

3:01:00 you so choose. Um and I think that is

3:01:04 strong evidence for the argument that

3:01:07 the comm you know the the the state

3:01:11 considers that to be a reasonable

3:01:13 penalty for what they call all other

3:01:15 offenses which I as I read the statute

3:01:17 it's not well written. It's basically

3:01:18 anything other than parking in front of

3:01:20 a hydrant which is more expensive. Um,

3:01:22 so we have the uh benefit of two

3:01:25 transportation board members gracing us

3:01:27 with their presence and I would like to

3:01:31 invite them to come up and address this

3:01:34 if they would like briefly.

3:01:41 Yeah, I don't want to step on your toes.

3:01:52 so go ahead introduce yourself and also

3:03:28 boards. Congratulations all first of all

3:03:34 Seriously. Um so uh Anthony um we also

3:03:38 wanted to go to 50. There was a lot of

3:03:40 transportation board for everything.

3:03:47 thought there was wisdom in having

3:03:50 somewhere to go 40 to 50 assuming the

3:03:53 home rule doesn't pass. We've had

3:03:55 various luck with home rules uh in the

3:03:57 past. So we we thought it was important

3:04:06 limit as to the amount of a search

3:04:15 reason why I think we wanted to stay at

3:04:17 Yeah. It's it's this it's this issue of

3:04:19 you can't raise the you can't put on the

3:04:21 search char the search charges under

3:04:23 this structure are you know are kind of

3:04:26 have follow the logical pattern $15

3:04:28 after 21 days $15 after it gets reported

3:04:30 to the registar. If you go up to 50 you

3:04:32 have less room to go up after 21 days

3:04:35 right. So there's less of an incentive

3:04:36 for them to pay on time because it's

3:04:41 gets it. I don't have to pay 70 bucks.

3:04:46 to grow um made sense to go to 40 except

3:14:12 So, now we move into the world of fees

3:14:14 as opposed to fines. Um, building permit

3:14:17 fees. Um,

3:14:18 the town of Brookline has not raised

3:14:20 building permit fees in recent memory.

3:14:22 Um, by record, Dan Bennett, our building

3:14:25 commissioner, is retiring this year

3:14:26 after many, many years of service. He

3:14:28 first came to the town in 1993. At that

3:14:31 point, building permit fees were still

3:14:33 $20 per $1,000 of construction value or

3:14:36 fraction thereof. Um, similarly,

3:14:39 certificates of occupancy were $50 per

3:14:41 dwelling unit. Um, since then,

3:14:44 obviously, the regulatory environment

3:14:45 has grown significantly more complex.

3:14:48 Um, there's a lot more for building

3:14:50 inspectors to do. there's a need for

3:14:52 their services more. They are working

3:14:53 longer hours. They're doing more complex

3:14:55 things. They are positions that are

3:14:57 being re-evaluated in terms of how we

3:15:00 pay them and whether we need additional

3:15:02 resources and so forth. Um so there is a

3:15:05 critical need for the fees to be

3:15:08 commensurate with the costs. Um and so a

3:15:12 slight increase in the building permit

3:15:14 fees is appropriate under the

3:15:16 circumstances we feel. Now I think the

3:15:18 obvious question is it's been since

3:15:19 1993. Yeah.

3:15:21 Why on earth is it only $1? And the

3:15:24 answer is because $1 adds up um per

3:15:28 thousand of construction value. And

3:15:30 right now, no other community has

3:15:32 breached that $20 cap. We would be the

3:15:34 first community to go to $21.

3:15:36 I'm sorry, I didn't mean to interrupt.

3:15:37 Yeah. No, it's okay.

3:15:38 No, I just want to say that fee

3:15:40 structure makes a lot more sense of

3:15:42 dollar per 10,000 of construction

3:15:44 because that's almost adjusting it for

3:15:46 inflation in of itself because

3:15:48 construction costs are increasing over

3:15:50 time. So that fee structure makes way

3:15:51 more sense and kind of alleviates the

3:15:53 inflation concern I had about the

3:15:55 parking fine.

3:15:56 Right. And in this case in particular,

3:15:57 we will be we will be pioneers here. To

3:16:00 be fair, we were pioneers last time.

3:16:04 Brookline's

3:16:05 fee structure and are now there. Um they

3:16:08 are now at $20 per thousand construction

3:16:10 value. Can you give an example just so

3:16:12 that I can wrap my head around what a

3:16:17 for I I don't know whatever example you

3:16:19 want to give. I just don't understand

3:16:21 per thousand. Well, I mean, so take, you

3:16:23 know, ordinarily, you know, the value of

3:16:26 the constru like the value of a home

3:16:27 renovation constru, you know,

3:16:28 construction project, it might be a

3:16:30 couple hundred dollars um for a minor

3:16:32 minor renovations and so forth. But when

3:16:35 you think of big projects like the

3:16:37 projects in Hancock Village, that was

3:16:39 hundreds of thousands of dollars um of

3:16:42 revenue to the town that then went into

3:16:45 free cash um and became a major source

3:16:47 of uh um revenue for the for the um

3:16:51 community because it exceeded the uh

3:16:53 expected uh revenue coming in from

3:16:55 building permits. So building permits

3:16:58 because they scale like that um the

3:17:00 bigger the project, the lumpier the

3:17:02 source of income. It is a highly

3:17:04 volatile source of income for the town.

3:17:06 If you look at the five-year average, it

3:17:08 bounces. Um, so

3:17:11 I just think it's interesting because I

3:17:12 remember three years ago, we were

3:17:14 talking about this huge influx of free

3:17:15 cash and it was because of building

3:17:16 permit fees. That's right. And at town

3:17:18 meeting there was a presentation where

3:17:19 we had a talk about, okay, we know free

3:17:21 cash is big right now, but it's because

3:17:22 these building permit fees, they won't

3:17:23 be there forever.

3:17:24 Right. Right.

3:17:25 Combination of building permit fees,

3:17:26 ARPA, the federal money being in the

3:17:28 bank and earning interest, you know,

3:17:30 there are all sorts of things on that

3:17:31 front. So yeah, I hope that answers your

3:17:34 question.

3:17:36 And the other aspect of this that the

3:17:37 building commissioner flagged is right

3:17:40 certificates of occupancy. Again, the

3:17:43 when someone asks for a certificate of

3:17:45 occupancy, applies for a certificate of

3:17:46 occupancy, there's a final inspection

3:17:47 that needs to take place. That's

3:17:49 significantly more complex than it used

3:17:51 to be. Um, and it costs more of the

3:17:53 inspector's time, more of the

3:17:54 inspector's effort. So, it makes sense

3:17:56 to bring this into the modern era and

3:17:59 say it's should be $100 a unit.

3:18:19 voted to increase fees for construction

3:18:22 related permits listed in the town

3:18:23 administrator's memo of May 8th, 2026

3:18:26 from $20 per $1,000 of construction

3:18:29 project value or fraction thereof to $21

3:18:32 per $1,000 of construction project value

3:18:35 or fraction thereof. and to increase

3:18:38 application fees for permanent and

3:18:40 temporary certificates of occupancy from

3:18:42 $50 per dwelling unit to $100 per

3:18:44 dwelling unit. Such fee increases to

3:18:46 take effect no earlier than July 1,

3:18:48 2026.

3:18:51 yes.

3:18:52 Michael,

3:18:52 yes.

3:24:07 Hi everyone. I'm Marissa Vote. I'm a

3:24:09 town meeting member in precinct 6. Um

3:24:12 nice to see you all and there some

3:24:14 musical chairs that have happened here

3:24:16 since the last time I was up here. Um

3:24:20 uh so um Andy Fischer and I have filed a

3:24:23 condition of appropriation on the

3:24:25 rehabilitation of streets budget item

3:24:28 that asks um uh what does it say? It

3:24:32 says um that any money spent on

3:24:35 rehabilitation of streets should um if

3:24:38 if the streets that are being worked on

3:24:40 are part of what's called the um high

3:24:42 priority network in the vision zero plan

3:24:45 um which those streets are the I think

3:24:47 28% of streets in Brooklyn that are the

3:24:51 identified as the highest priority

3:24:53 because they have um I think it's like

3:24:55 100% of the um pedestrian crashes and

3:24:59 and um it's there's there's text in

3:25:02 there, but these are these are the

3:25:03 streets that have been identified as the

3:25:04 highest priority for vision zero

3:25:06 treatments um to uh save lives for um

3:25:10 when there's a vehicle crash. And um so

3:25:13 if any work is being done on those

3:25:15 streets, then um that that work should

3:25:19 include the vision zero treatments

3:25:21 unless approved by the transportation

3:25:23 board. Um, so the the reason for um for

3:25:28 saying this is because we want to make

3:25:30 sure that um we want to make sure that

3:25:32 these vision zero treatments are are

3:25:35 moving forward. Uh we want to make sure

3:25:37 that if we're doing work on these high

3:25:38 priority streets that we are um

3:25:40 servicing all road users and every um uh

3:25:46 mode of transportation in our community.

3:25:48 Um, we do not want to bind town staff's

3:25:53 hands. We don't want to make it

3:25:55 difficult for them to um to work on

3:25:59 these streets, but we do want to make

3:26:00 sure that there's a public meeting from

3:26:02 the transportation board and a a public

3:26:04 process um where we're weighing the

3:26:07 costs and benefits of doing those vision

3:26:10 zero treatments that have been

3:26:12 recommended. Um, and you know, maybe it

3:26:14 doesn't make sense to install all of the

3:26:18 protected bike lanes or safety humps or

3:26:19 something, whatever it is. Um, but that

3:26:22 that decision would be made at a public

3:26:24 meeting of the transportation board

3:26:26 where we hear the the costbenefit

3:26:27 analysis and then the transportation

3:26:29 board could say, you know, it at this

3:26:32 time it doesn't make sense to put in

3:26:34 that that bike lane. We're going to wait

3:26:36 a couple of years. Um, but the idea is

3:26:39 that there would have to be a decision

3:26:42 not to do those things and that's the

3:26:45 gist.

3:26:46 Now, the $100,000 threshold, do we know

3:26:49 what percentage of projects and

3:26:52 repavings that would capture?

3:26:59 um relates to the $100,000 threshold,

3:32:34 uh Paul Warren?

3:32:39 Paul Warren, town meeting member from

3:32:41 Precinct One. First, I do uh want to

3:32:44 congratulate Amanda and Anthony on their

3:32:47 election. uh welcome to the select board

3:32:50 uh and Bernard for your service uh and

3:32:53 your uh your your work and partnership

3:32:56 over the years and David and Michael for

3:32:58 your leadership roles. Um Amy's

3:33:01 wondering where I where I am tonight. I

3:33:03 thought you were done. Um it's not true.

3:33:07 Um I do want to I have some prepared

3:33:09 remarks just to keep me on track. Um I I

3:33:12 first uh do want to recognize that the

3:33:16 petitioners that brought this forward uh

3:33:18 the the amendment that you just heard um

3:33:21 uh the Andy and and Marissa you know

3:33:25 their amendment does identify something

3:33:27 that's really important and that's our

3:33:30 vision our commitment to vision zero um

3:33:33 and highlighting that we haven't yet

3:33:35 really figured out how to integrate that

3:33:37 into our roadway um and how to co

3:33:41 coordinate our street work with those

3:33:43 with the investments in our streets with

3:33:46 um with the vision zero program. Um by

3:33:49 the way, this amendment is brought

3:33:50 forward by the chair of the

3:33:52 transportation board who's standing next

3:33:54 to me. Uh and also the former chair of

3:33:56 the vision zero committee, Michael

3:33:58 Sandman. Um so we're kind of partners um

3:34:02 in crime on this. But I do want to uh

3:34:03 acknowledge that I think we have a

3:34:05 common goal in mind here and really what

3:34:08 we're doing is coming forward to see if

3:34:09 there's a path forward that can

3:34:11 accommodate the issue that they're

3:34:12 raising but also come up with a solution

3:34:15 uh that could be kind of proactive and

3:34:17 and and systemic. Um, I do want to

3:34:20 highlight that, uh, Brian, Michael,

3:34:23 Sammon, and I two and a half years ago,

3:34:26 uh, met with Commissioner Chute, um,

3:34:29 our, uh, transportation engineers, our

3:34:32 consultants, and we spent a lot of time

3:34:34 looking at complete streets and the the

3:34:36 various set of policies that affect our

3:34:38 roadways with the intention of figuring

3:34:41 out how to rationalize our our

3:34:43 investments. And over the past 18

3:34:45 months, the select board and the

3:34:47 transportation board has adopted four

3:34:50 policies uh related to this and

3:34:52 governing how our streets are planned,

3:34:54 maintained uh and made safer. They

3:34:57 include the traffic calming policy, the

3:35:00 roadway asset management, maintenance

3:35:02 and preservation policy, otherwise known

3:35:04 as the uh ARP, the no the the RAMP, the

3:35:07 ramp is the acronym for that. Our

3:35:10 complete streets policy and our vision

3:35:12 zero action plan. Together, they

3:35:14 represent a genuine commitment for safer

3:35:17 streets. But these four policies don't

3:35:19 currently work together in a systemic

3:35:22 way. Vision Zero calls for safety

3:35:24 treatments on up to 28% of our streets.

3:35:27 And this is defined, we've heard about,

3:35:29 as the high priority network. The

3:35:31 traffic calming policy prioritizes

3:35:33 streets using crash data and v uh and

3:35:36 vulnerable road user exposure. Yet, the

3:35:38 ramp and complete streets explicitly

3:35:41 require that safety and calming

3:35:43 investments be funded and designed on

3:35:45 separate tracks from pavement

3:35:47 rehabilitation. So, the traffic calming

3:35:50 policy even acknowledges this tension,

3:35:53 noting that its own procedures do not

3:35:55 apply when large complex uh complete

3:35:58 street projects are involved. The result

3:36:00 is a gap and there's no owner for that

3:36:03 gap. Uh, a corridor can be repaved and

3:36:05 the safety investments vision zero calls

3:36:08 for on the same street can be left

3:36:10 behind and that's a problem. um not

3:36:12 because anyone chose to ignore them, but

3:36:14 because there's really no process in

3:36:16 place uh to connect the two. And I think

3:36:18 that's what the petitioners of the the

3:36:20 other amendment are trying to do.

3:36:22 They're trying to do it through funing

3:36:24 everything through uh through the

3:36:25 transportation board. So, this amendment

3:36:28 that uh that we're bringing forward, um

3:36:30 the amendment doesn't slow down paving

3:36:33 or restrict how this uh appropriation is

3:36:35 spent. The 5.2 2 million still proceeds

3:36:38 in full uh under the authority of the

3:36:41 commissioner of public works. But what

3:36:43 it does add is one condition that the

3:36:45 DPW and the transportation board form a

3:36:49 working group. That would be the

3:36:51 trigger. That's the condition to develop

3:36:53 a prioritization and communication

3:36:55 process that integrates all four

3:36:58 policies into a coherent framework. one

3:37:01 that identifies priorities, aligns

3:37:03 expectations, supports public

3:37:05 engagement, and allows rehabilitation

3:37:08 work to proceed in a timely and

3:37:09 cost-effective manner. The benefits

3:37:12 pretty straightforward. Residents will

3:37:13 know not just when their street will be

3:37:15 repaved or redone, but what safety

3:37:18 improvements are coming and how those

3:37:19 decisions are being made. DPW and the

3:37:22 transportation board will have a clear

3:37:23 shared process for coordinating

3:37:25 investments rather than managing each

3:37:27 policy in isolation. And Town Meeting

3:37:30 will be better positioned to fund those

3:37:31 programs with confidence. And Brooklyn

3:37:34 has the ability to build to take these

3:37:36 strong policies that we've already

3:37:38 developed and integrate them into uh a

3:37:41 process and a solution that's much more

3:37:43 transparent uh to the community and also

3:37:45 making our roadways safe without slowing

3:37:48 down and stopping uh this important work

3:37:50 that is to maintain our roadways. And as

3:37:52 I think someone said earlier, maybe it

3:37:54 was Michael at the beginning of the

3:37:55 meeting, Michael Sammon said, you know,

3:37:57 it's dangerous to drive down our roads

3:37:59 when there's a pothole and a crack

3:38:00 that's not filled and you know, these

3:38:02 things that get in our way. It would be

3:38:04 nice if it was a protected bike lane,

3:38:05 but even having a smooth surface would

3:38:07 be a great uh a great improvement. So,

3:38:10 that's the amendment that we're asking

3:38:11 for your consideration. and it's more

3:38:13 processoriented

3:38:14 still uh creates a trigger for the

3:38:16 appropriation and we'll put the work in

3:38:19 place to make sure that for the next CIP

3:38:21 we'll have those procedures in place.

3:38:23 Did you want to say something M?

3:38:25 Uh if I might. Uh thank you. Uh Brian

3:38:27 Kaine, chair of the transportation

3:38:28 board. Um the reason I am a co-itioner

3:38:32 on this amendment and let me just echo

3:38:34 everything Paul said. Um we we agree uh

3:38:37 I I Marissa and I spoke before we all

3:38:40 want to get to the same place which is

3:38:41 safer streets in Brooklyn. That's the

3:38:43 goal. It's really a question of how do

3:38:44 we get there. The reason why I am not in

3:38:48 support of the original amendment and

3:38:50 supporting the revised amendment or what

3:38:52 we're calling this um the administrative

3:38:55 burden that would be put on my board is

3:38:58 is simply almost unworkable. Um we do

3:39:02 not have the staff support that the

3:39:03 select board has. We meet monthly. Um,

3:39:06 and getting my folks to meet monthly is

3:39:10 not always easy with with folks

3:39:12 schedules. I'm not telling you folks

3:39:13 anything you don't know. But the

3:39:15 administrative burden that would be

3:39:16 placed on my board to try and oversee

3:39:18 and approve and have a public meeting on

3:39:20 every single expenditure over $100,000,

3:39:22 which we've just heard is almost every

3:39:24 single project. I is simply too much

3:39:26 with the current resources and the

3:39:28 current structures that we have right

3:39:29 now. We I want to get to the same place

3:39:32 that the original petitioners want us to

3:39:34 get to. We share that same vision, safer

3:39:37 roads. We are going through a

3:39:38 prioritization process. When I heard the

3:39:40 work that you folks were doing with your

3:39:42 2030 roadmap, it sounds very similar to

3:39:45 the work that we're doing in the

3:39:46 transportation board, trying to merge

3:39:48 and meld all these things together and

3:39:49 find a path forward that is proactive

3:39:51 and very clear to everyone who's looking

3:39:53 in as to why we're doing what we're

3:39:55 doing. We all want to get to the same

3:39:57 place. I'm just asking you to please not

3:39:58 place this this immense administrative

3:40:01 burden on on the transportation board.

3:40:04 It really will harm our ability to get

3:40:06 things done. Thank you.

3:40:10 Any questions, input from other members?

3:40:13 Amanda,

3:40:14 question. So, I I do really appreciate

3:40:17 the process argument. I think it would

3:40:19 be great to have a cohesive process. Um,

3:40:22 and I'm glad that you're putting that in

3:40:24 as something to do. My worry is that if

3:40:28 um if the version that Marissa and Andy

3:40:31 didn't put forward doesn't pass, there's

3:40:34 nothing that influences the road work

3:40:36 that's happening in the next year. And

3:40:40 when this process will finish, when that

3:40:43 will influence the next decisions on CIP

3:40:46 funding for roads. Um so I think I I do

3:40:52 not know how to wordsmith this. I would

3:40:54 love to have a situation where we could

3:40:56 do process and address the more

3:41:10 action or high priority network in

3:41:17 process stuff.

3:41:21 100%. Uh there we will be done with our

3:41:26 we've begun the we we've started this in

3:41:38 know what I'm talking about. Harvard

3:41:39 Street. Uh and that's unfortunate and I

3:41:47 again. I don't think any of us want that

3:41:48 to happen again.

3:41:54 on the scoring in the rubric that has

3:41:59 whole presentation about uh will

3:42:02 absolutely seemingly put most of the

3:42:04 high priority streets that are on the

3:42:32 corridors I agree with you and as I said

3:42:34 I think we all want to get to the same

3:42:37 do we get there

3:42:40 and Amanda I would add one thing thanks

3:42:42 Brian that again you know this this work

3:42:45 is been uncovered that it's going right

3:42:47 it's already it's already started but I

3:43:10 know, if if the transportation board and

3:43:38 Michael.

3:44:26 Thank you very much. Uh great question.

3:44:28 Erin Chude, Commissioner of Public

3:44:30 Works. And uh just by way of a little

3:44:34 bit of background, it was probably four

3:44:37 years ago where the select board um

3:44:41 really put maintenance of the town's

3:44:44 roads at the top of their priority list

3:44:48 and directed public works to to develop

3:44:51 a strategy to um maintain these assets.

3:44:56 And so um you heard about uh this

3:44:58 working group with uh select board

3:45:01 members, chair of the transportation

3:45:02 board to try to figure out how do we

3:45:04 maintain our roads and yet also at the s

3:45:07 same time push forward and prioritize

3:45:10 all of our complete streets uh work. So

3:45:14 we have in terms of our roadway um

3:45:18 maintenance and management plan uh and

3:45:21 that's supported by this roadway asset

3:45:23 preservation maintenance and management

3:45:25 policy. Uh a conditions assessment that

3:45:28 is uh done on the roads uh we've been

3:45:31 doing it about every 3 years. We just

3:45:33 completed it and it uses artificial

3:45:36 intelligence. It actually takes um

3:45:39 images of our entire roadway network and

3:45:41 applies a um value condition value 0 to

3:45:45 100 on every block segment. Uh that's

3:45:49 all been mapped and we're presenting all

3:45:52 of that at the transportation board uh

3:45:55 tomorrow evening. So I welcome you to

3:45:57 attend. We also presented this

3:45:58 information to the advisory committee uh

3:46:02 during our budgeting process this year.

3:46:04 We presented it last year to the select

3:46:06 board. There have been some um

3:46:08 modifications and and updates uh with

3:46:10 the new data that we have, but we've

3:46:13 really every year we've been um

3:46:15 presenting that through the budget

3:46:16 process to show what that 5-year plan

3:46:18 looks like and what the conditions

3:46:20 analysis look looks like. And we have a

3:46:22 dashboard on our um web page where you

3:46:25 can actually see the map um with those

3:46:28 segments. Uh what we're looking to do is

3:46:32 to annually present this full um asset

3:46:36 management plan for maintenance to the

3:46:39 transportation board and then move into

3:46:42 our prioritization

3:46:43 um process for the transportation CIP

3:46:46 projects. Um so we'll look at traffic

3:46:49 calming pl projects, we'll look at

3:46:51 vision zero, we'll look at our complete

3:46:53 streets. We have a whole slew of of

3:46:56 really important transformative projects

3:46:58 that are underway now. And then we want

3:47:00 to have sort of design and construction

3:47:02 moving forward each year within the

3:47:05 5-year CIP so that everyone can see

3:47:07 what's coming up. We also have developed

3:47:10 guidelines for community engagement. So

3:47:13 all of this needs to come together. It

3:47:15 is uh going to be a new way for the

3:47:17 transportation board and transportation

3:47:20 staff to approach and communicate all of

3:47:23 these projects proactively. Um I want to

3:47:28 thank Marissa who reached out to me uh

3:47:31 with the amendment and wanted to talk

3:47:33 about how do we do this? You know DPDW

3:47:36 understands uh the goals behind this

3:47:38 amendment. Vision zero complete streets

3:47:41 our bike safety accommodations all of

3:47:43 that. They're not only town goals, but

3:47:46 um many of this DPW led the way uh and

3:47:50 partnered with um the community in terms

3:47:53 of writing, managing, and leading these

3:47:55 policies. So, we're very much on board.

3:47:58 I think it's really not an issue of

3:48:00 where we're trying to go. It's we agree

3:48:03 on the destination. The issue is how we

3:48:05 realistically get there. And um

3:48:08 historically the town has been in a

3:48:09 place where it really has um been very

3:48:12 difficult to move forward. Um

3:48:14 rehabilitation and maintenance in terms

3:48:16 of asset management is different and

3:48:20 distinct from um infrastructure changes

3:48:23 or changes operationally around how we

3:48:25 share the road. And that's why we're

3:48:27 putting this um sort of clarity forward

3:48:30 in terms of community engagement because

3:48:34 while it all seems um very

3:48:36 straightforward

3:48:38 um Washington Street for example which

3:48:39 is a major um major construction

3:48:42 initiative with a lot of changes in

3:48:44 curve line but that community engagement

3:48:48 process and the need to really work

3:48:50 through all the issues added two years

3:48:54 um to um when we're going to put a

3:48:56 shovel in the ground on that project.

3:48:58 And so we just want to make sure we're

3:49:00 not losing the overall condition of the

3:49:04 assets um because we need to maintain

3:49:07 this in a state of good repair. And that

3:49:09 is equally important for safety, for

3:49:12 liability, and for um the town's uh

3:49:17 financial solveny. Because if we let the

3:49:19 roads degrade to a po point where they

3:49:21 are all full reconstructions, it will be

3:49:24 incredibly ownorous for this town and we

3:49:27 really won't even then be in a position

3:49:29 to move forward with u multimmoal

3:49:32 projects. We're just going to be hanging

3:49:33 on uh to keep our roads. So I think it's

3:49:37 I think it's really important that we

3:49:39 have this process but I don't think we

3:49:41 really I don't think we should conflate

3:49:44 uh basic maintenance and operations

3:49:48 and asset management with our bigger

3:49:52 picture vision zero complete streets

3:49:54 multimodal

3:49:55 and I think another sort of just little

3:49:59 point because we had the the question

3:50:00 around the crack sealing you know crack

3:50:02 sealing our roads is helping prevent

3:50:05 vent um water infiltration. So as you

3:50:08 see seams opening, they can be large,

3:50:09 they can be small. So it's preserving

3:50:11 the longevity. The patching does the

3:50:13 same. The potholes and filling the

3:50:15 potholes does the same. And when we have

3:50:18 those contracts, it's like a pruning

3:50:20 contract for trees. We don't know

3:50:22 exactly how many tree, you know, which

3:50:24 trees we'll be pruning through the

3:50:26 course of the year, but we know on

3:50:27 average the volume of trees that we're

3:50:29 going to need to prune and the capacity

3:50:32 that we have both in terms of funding

3:50:34 and staff to do that. So, it's it's

3:50:36 similar uh in terms of um preventative

3:50:41 maintenance and best practices.

3:50:44 Michael,

3:50:45 um first of all, thank you very much. Um

3:50:48 and this is this is obviously uh a lot

3:50:52 of thought has been put into how to

3:50:54 improve our processes and also how to

3:50:56 communicate them. Um, I think that one

3:51:00 of the challenges that was uncovered in

3:51:02 the Harvard street process was the fact

3:51:05 that the timing of the of the

3:51:09 maintenance work um, relative to the

3:51:13 community's understanding of that timing

3:51:15 and their their ability to identify

3:51:24 some uh, small safety improvements ments

3:51:28 um that are that are incorporated into

3:51:33 that there was a challenge within the

3:51:35 community of understanding that timing

3:51:37 and understanding the capacity to impact

3:51:47 to meet our vision zero goals. And so

3:52:31 RAMP

3:52:33 and I I just want to make sure that

3:52:38 um so that people can understand that

3:52:40 and weigh in appropriately through the

3:52:42 transportation board process. processes

3:52:45 um that are already in place and are

3:52:46 being developed in the course of this

3:52:48 prioritization.

3:52:51 Yep. Absolutely. So that will um they'll

3:52:56 be presented. I think that they're clear

3:52:58 in terms of it's in a list format with

3:53:02 the streets and a year. There's a little

3:53:04 caveat in terms of outy years um are

3:53:08 subject to change. Uh you know, so we're

3:53:11 going to revisit this every year.

3:53:12 there's development work, utilities

3:53:14 work, um you know, other things may come

3:53:18 up that that cause a shift in uh the

3:53:21 roads that will um you know, be moving

3:53:28 And uh as you mentioned, there are we

3:53:31 think a lot of safety improvements that

3:53:34 are happening as part of the Harvard

3:53:38 street uh rehabilitation project that

3:53:42 don't change the way people share the

3:53:45 road um but puts more protections in

3:53:48 place um in terms of you know the better

3:53:51 striping and ballers and daylighting

3:54:12 to apply and move forward. Um when we

3:54:22 time. um we're gonna have to build in

3:54:35 those other um improvements uh that

3:54:38 we're looking to build. So that's

3:54:40 something that that's why we're working

3:54:41 to develop this CIP

3:54:44 um together with the T board. So not

3:54:46 only is it transparent, but we're

3:54:48 understanding the time it's going to

3:54:49 take and the dollars it's going to take

3:55:51 Agree.

3:55:52 Can I just um add one more uh thing? You

3:56:16 understand how the board feels about it.

3:56:18 All right. So, given that constraint,

3:56:20 I'll take that recommendation. I think

3:56:23 that uh both amendments are are very

3:56:27 well-intentioned to have the same goal

3:56:29 in mind, just different paths on how to

3:56:31 get there. I found uh Brian Kaine's

3:56:34 remarks as transportation share quite

3:56:38 compelling that this would be a

3:56:39 tremendous administrative burden on his

3:56:42 board to have essentially everything

3:56:44 road related funneled through the

3:56:46 transportation board. Uh by no means is

3:56:49 that an indictment on this vote, Mr.

3:56:51 Fischer who again have the same goal in

3:56:54 mind as Paul Warren, Brian Kaine, and

3:56:56 Mike Sandman who used to chair the

3:56:59 vision zero uh committee. So obviously

3:57:02 this is uh a shared goal by all. So I

3:57:05 would like us to support the amendment

3:57:09 brought to us by Paul Warren, Brian

3:57:11 Kaine, and Mike Sandman. Uh and

3:57:14 certainly I agree with you Amanda that

3:57:15 we want to make sure that this isn't

3:57:18 just talk but that there's action that

3:57:20 goes along with it and the way it's

3:57:22 structured there is that uh trigger as a

3:57:28 minimum this working group is created

3:57:30 and gets started on on work they've

3:57:33 already done informally to an extent but

3:57:36 this would formalize that process and I

3:57:38 think that's a good approach to take

3:57:39 there is also nothing that would

3:57:41 preclude further conversation s between

3:57:44 the uh petitioners of the uh Fiser vote

3:57:48 amendment and the Warren Kaine Sandman

3:57:52 amendment from perhaps further

3:57:53 refinement as we approach town meeting.

3:57:56 Uh as even our two uh new members well

3:57:59 know even on the day of town meeting

3:58:02 sometimes we have additional votes and

3:58:04 changes if those uh become necessary.

3:58:07 So, I would like to move the uh

3:58:10 favorable action on the Warren Kaine

3:58:13 Sandman amendment.

4:14:20 Good evening everybody again. Uh Tim

4:14:22 Davis, recreation director for the town

4:14:23 of Brooklyn. I am joined tonight by

4:14:25 Justin Lawson, general manager over at

4:14:27 the golf course to help answer any

4:14:29 questions that you'll have tonight after

4:14:30 our conversation. Um, and again, caught

4:14:33 me off guard earlier just like everybody

4:14:34 else. So, I echo everybody's compliments

4:14:36 tonight. Um, thank you Bernard for your

4:14:38 guidance and participation over the

4:14:40 years with the rec department and Mr.

4:14:42 Brono, Dr. Zimmerman, we look forward to

4:14:44 working with you and welcoming you into

4:14:46 the recreation department. Dr. Zimmerman

4:14:48 being a frequent flyer already, so glad

4:14:50 to see you again. Um so as mentioned in

4:14:53 my memo in response to the advisory

4:14:55 amendment

4:14:57 um and the purpose of my note to to shed

4:14:59 light on arate um which is the requested

4:15:02 transfer and allocation of funds from

4:15:04 the golf course retained earnings for

4:15:06 future future capital planning in this

4:15:08 case being the potential for lighting

4:15:10 the golf course driving range to expand

4:15:12 that recreational opportunity in town.

4:15:15 But um even just as important um ensures

4:15:19 further financial stability of the

4:15:21 course. Um it as mentioned late last

4:15:25 month uh the advisory board voted to

4:15:28 strike the warrant article and took a

4:15:29 vote of no action on the transfer. Uh

4:15:32 that was due to concerns expressed about

4:15:34 the project and perceived inaction of

4:15:36 the department to address the initial

4:15:38 concerns that the the community had. Um

4:15:41 and also the want to have a more robust

4:15:44 public process. Um this project from the

4:15:47 beginning has been and continues to be a

4:15:50 planned uh public process. Our first

4:15:52 meeting was um notified our our first

4:15:56 meeting included notification to

4:15:57 abutters uh notification to neighbors um

4:16:00 and included significant public comment

4:16:02 um and our future meetings will do the

4:16:04 same um until we seek final support from

4:16:07 the park and rec commission on this

4:16:08 project to move forward. Um really the

4:16:11 concerns that were brought up and shared

4:16:12 by the committee uh and also residents

4:16:15 in the neighborhood regarding the

4:16:16 impacts um of this project such as dark

4:16:19 skies compliance and environmental

4:16:20 impacts were valid um and heard and the

4:16:24 department's been working Justin and I

4:16:26 um and our whole department really have

4:16:27 been working on these concerns since

4:16:29 they were shared to us uh in December.

4:16:31 Um and we recognize that we're now in

4:16:33 May. Um, and the explanation for that,

4:16:36 as was explained at advisory, simply

4:16:38 comes down to the fact that the actual

4:16:41 lighting plan from the vendor we have is

4:16:43 not to our satisfaction. Um, and not to

4:16:46 a level where we know the town would

4:16:47 find it favorable. Um, the delay has

4:16:50 been unexpected and we're doing all we

4:16:52 can to work through that process. um

4:16:56 because at the last thing we want to do

4:16:57 is is bring back something to the town

4:16:59 that's that's incomplete or or causing

4:17:01 more more uh questions than than

4:17:04 answers. Um this project also, as a

4:17:06 reminder, is is funded out of the golf

4:17:08 course enterprise retained earnings, so

4:17:10 no impact to the town's operating fund.

4:17:12 um seeking the funding now um prior to

4:17:15 the final approvals of the project um as

4:17:18 is customary with most capital items um

4:17:21 just um allows us the timeline to

4:17:23 procure what what is needed. Um so if

4:17:26 those funds are released this this town

4:17:27 meeting u making them available for us

4:17:30 to move forward on the project. um if

4:17:32 this project is found unfavorable um

4:17:34 that money would simply go back into

4:17:36 retained earnings of the golf course um

4:17:38 and be spent on further future

4:17:40 operations or or future future capital.

4:17:43 Um so really we're the the concerns

4:17:46 brought forward around that public

4:17:48 process and around the response of of

4:17:50 what we're working on um are are really

4:17:53 all being met and and being planned. Um,

4:17:56 so you know, we just wanted to to offer

4:17:58 our our side of it. Um, as I know you

4:18:01 need to take a position, as Melissa

4:18:02 mentioned, getting close to town

4:18:04 meeting. Um, we're trying our best to be

4:18:07 good stewards of of the ground, good

4:18:08 stewards of the property, good stewards

4:18:10 of the neighborhood, um, while expanding

4:18:12 these opportunities in town. Um, and I

4:18:17 you want to make any comments, Justin,

4:18:19 related to that. But again, here to

4:18:23 piece.

4:18:26 Yeah, Tim, thank you very much. Uh

4:18:29 folks, uh Justin Lawson, uh general

4:18:31 manager at the Brooklyn Golf Course. Um

4:18:46 uh we just haven't uh found the solution

4:19:44 it's not common for a project because

4:19:51 that is in the process of being followed

4:48:44 meeting to start thinking about how they

4:48:46 get awarded.

4:48:48 The the other thing u my question which

4:48:50 I know the answer to is what does the uh

4:48:53 CPA committee think of this idea? Do

4:48:56 they have an argument for or again? I

4:49:03 is supportive of dividing the question,

4:49:04 I think that that would probably change

4:49:08 going forward.

4:49:32 if the motion is going to be made every

4:50:06 So first

4:50:50 Okay. uh

4:50:52 you know there's there's uh there's

4:50:54 requirements when you do but if it's

4:50:56 just simply um that I wasn't here before

4:51:00 you can rely on kind of the good faith

4:51:03 of the board before or the staff uh in

4:51:06 preparing minutes but you're not

4:51:07 obligated to abstain just because you

4:51:10 weren't um you know at the meeting

4:51:12 before. There is what's called the

4:51:14 Mullen rule which applies to planning

4:51:16 boards and kind of permitting but that

4:51:18 doesn't apply. Um, so my advice is if

4:51:22 you can, you know, trust the staff and

4:58:08 Thank you. Froley, the petitioner uh for

4:58:11 13.

4:58:13 I'm keeping my article as a bylaw for

4:58:16 now, but I've been very pleased with

4:58:19 Harry Boes and the efforts on the part

4:58:21 of the advisory committee to push this

4:58:24 along because the consensus was

4:58:28 evening hours. The the compromise was to

4:58:33 give the clerk a lot more time to get it

4:58:35 together. Uh I haven't heard a word from

4:58:37 the clerk since I've done this, so this

4:58:40 is the first I'm hearing he's even

4:58:41 online.

4:58:43 So, if there's any questions, I'd be

4:58:45 glad to answer.

4:58:47 Michael,

4:58:48 so I just want to make sure I understood

4:58:50 what you said. Um, did you you said

4:58:59 I'm keeping my options open.

4:59:05 to Harry's amendment because they wanted

4:59:08 me to keep the um keep it as a bylaw.

4:59:14 and you do what you're going to do.

4:59:16 Okay.

4:59:17 But I'm very pleased with the efforts

4:59:19 that uh Harry has made.

4:59:21 All right. We we have the town clerk on.

4:59:24 Uh Ben, if we could have your opinion on

4:59:26 the Warren article, your thoughts.

4:59:30 Uh sure. I I I shared much of my uh my

4:59:32 thoughts in the uh memo that I uh sent

4:59:35 to the select board. I think a couple

4:59:36 weeks ago, you guys had a meeting

4:59:38 shortly before the election. and I was

4:59:39 unable to attend due to it conflicting

4:59:41 with the deadline for vote by mail

4:59:42 ballots uh to be sent out. Um but uh in

4:59:46 the memo I outlined some of the cost

4:59:48 constraints uh that this would impose on

4:59:50 the clerk's office. Um even if it was a

4:59:52 situation where we simply offered

4:59:53 overtime to staff um it would run close

4:59:56 to $10,000 and a budget that my office

4:59:58 currently doesn't have. Um as well as

5:00:00 some of these staffing constraints. uh

5:00:02 would be likely subject to collective

5:00:04 bargaining and there's been a general

5:00:05 shift among staff uh away from being

5:00:08 interested in uh taking overtime even

5:00:10 when it is paid at that overtime rate.

5:00:12 So having staff available for these

5:00:14 evening hours uh would be uh a burden on

5:00:17 the clerk's office. Um at the request of

5:00:19 the advisory committee, I reached out to

5:00:20 a number of neighboring and peer

5:00:22 communities um to ask them how they

5:00:24 operate evening hours. Um num a number

5:00:27 of our neighboring communities, Boston,

5:00:29 uh Newton, they do not offer evening

5:00:31 hours. Uh Welssley used to offer evening

5:00:33 hours, but they saw low utilization. So

5:00:35 they've actually stopped offer offering

5:00:37 evening hours as well. And those

5:00:39 communities I was able to speak to uh

5:00:41 which were mostly peer communities uh

5:00:43 Belmont, Plymouth, Dam, Arlington, and

5:00:45 Lexington uh all shared three things in

5:00:48 common. Uh the first was that in no

5:00:51 situation is only the clerk's office

5:00:53 open uh evening hours. In many cases,

5:00:55 it's actually the entire town hall, or

5:00:57 at least many departments within town

5:00:58 hall. In all of those communities, they

5:01:01 uh accommodated this by adjusting the

5:01:03 operating hours of town hall itself,

5:01:05 often either opening later, closing

5:01:07 earlier, or being closed entirely on

5:01:09 Fridays. Um, in all of these

5:01:12 communities, the clerks uh when they

5:01:14 corresponded with me indicated that

5:01:16 utilization of evening hours at the

5:01:18 clerk's office was actually very low. Uh

5:01:20 we've put a number of our services now

5:01:21 are available online or available

5:01:24 through the Dropbox um and don't require

5:01:26 a visit to town hall um and that they've

5:01:28 seen in their communities um that

5:01:30 there's more utilization for example in

5:01:32 the treasurer's office paying bills or

5:01:34 with the human resources department

5:01:36 doing um onboarding work um but they're

5:01:38 not seeing that kind of traffic in the

5:01:40 clerk's office. Um, so I'm happy

5:01:42 obviously to explore uh the possibility

5:01:45 to have conversations with the union

5:01:47 about what those potential costs could

5:01:49 be. Um, but we want to make sure that we

5:01:51 are uh allocating our very limited

5:01:54 budget um to the highest needs and the

5:01:56 highest uh ways that we can serve serve

5:01:58 the public. Um, one other note that I

5:02:01 will have, there will likely be other

5:02:02 knock-on costs, um, that are also worth

5:02:04 exploring. Um, both in terms of the

5:02:06 actual application, uh, you know, things

5:02:08 like having to, uh, have, uh, electric

5:02:11 costs for extra hours the town hall is

5:02:13 open, custodial costs, um, potential

5:02:15 safety concerns of having, uh, the town

5:02:17 hall open with only one department

5:02:18 present. Um, as well as concerns for uh

5:02:21 how the CL clerk's office is going to

5:02:23 staff some of its other

5:02:24 responsibilities, especially related to

5:02:26 things like elections uh and town

5:02:28 meeting where staff is already uh

5:02:31 working uh excessive amounts of overtime

5:02:34 um and uh working quite extensively uh

5:02:36 not even at town hall, often over at the

5:02:38 high school during town meeting um and

5:02:40 how that can be uh accommodated within

5:02:43 uh a requirement of having two staff in

5:02:44 the clerk's office uh away from those

5:02:46 responsibilities. So, I certainly hear

5:02:50 the the concern, the request. Um, I'm

5:02:52 proud of the efforts we've made uh in

5:02:54 the clerk's office since COVID to make

5:02:56 sure our resources are available online.

5:02:59 Um, there is only one item that the

5:03:01 clerk's office uh one service the

5:03:02 clerk's office has available to the

5:03:04 public that requires in-person

5:03:06 attendance. Uh, and that is filing a

5:03:07 marriage intention. Um, other than that,

5:03:09 uh, we've put everything online.

5:03:11 Everything is available by Dropbox and

5:03:13 I'm very proud of the work that's been

5:03:14 done there. Um, so that's some of the

5:03:16 background on it and obviously I'm happy

5:03:17 to answer any of the board's questions.

5:03:19 So could you expand a little on the

5:03:22 overtime ramifications and costs of

5:03:26 having evening hours if for example

5:03:29 uh Brookline adopted the model where you

5:03:32 town hall opens later uh where it's the

5:03:35 same number of hours. So it's not

5:03:36 necessarily adding hours onto a person's

5:03:39 workload, but shifting them around and

5:03:41 maybe staggered start times. Sure. So,

5:03:45 uh, so if there was a change wholesale

5:03:47 in the operating hours of town hall, um,

5:03:50 so that staff stayed at their, you know,

5:03:52 regular within the regular confines of

5:03:53 working hours, uh, I would have to speak

5:03:55 with the union. I'm not sure if having

5:03:56 one longer day might impact bargaining

5:03:59 because, uh, my understanding is that

5:04:01 overtime can kick in after a certain

5:04:02 number of hours worked in a day. Um,

5:04:05 but, uh, that would require a full shift

5:04:07 in the timing of a town hall. Um, it's

5:04:10 been kind of generally understood during

5:04:12 the advisory committee and and I believe

5:04:14 this very much so that having the town

5:04:16 clerk's office open at the same hours as

5:04:18 the rest of town hall is very important.

5:04:20 Um, I don't think it would be

5:04:21 appropriate, for example, uh, for the

5:04:23 town clerk's office to open at 11:00

5:04:25 a.m. on Thursday and stay open late uh,

5:04:28 while the rest of town hall is still

5:04:29 open at 8 a.m. Um, because you'll then

5:04:31 have three hours where uh, people might

5:04:32 come in and we actually do see a decent

5:04:34 amount of usage uh, in the morning. and

5:04:36 that's typically our busiest time in the

5:04:37 clerk's office. Coming in with the

5:04:39 expectation the clerk's office is open

5:04:40 and it is not otherwise. Um so reducing

5:04:43 hours of the operations of town hall is

5:04:46 a much larger conversation and one that

5:04:48 uh would involve much more than my

5:04:50 department. Um but uh uh that would be a

5:04:53 way to do this without having to do the

5:04:55 overtime within the current requirements

5:04:57 of our operating hours 8 to 5 Monday

5:04:59 through Thursday 8 to 12:30 on Friday.

5:05:01 Um it would be imposing additional

5:05:03 overtime. And my understanding when this

5:05:05 was implemented before was that uh staff

5:05:07 were paid overtime for working those

5:05:09 extra hours.

5:05:11 What about staggered hours for

5:05:13 employees? So it's longer hours in

5:05:16 general that the clerk's office is open,

5:05:18 but for any individual employee, their

5:05:21 hours aren't changing. So you'd have two

5:05:22 shifts within the same day, for example.

5:05:25 Certainly. One of the reasons why we do

5:05:27 not like doing that is because of the

5:05:29 volume of work that comes into the

5:05:31 clerk's office. We typically see our

5:05:33 largest uh amount of visits, whether

5:05:36 it's at the counter, on the phone, uh

5:05:38 happen in the morning between the hours

5:05:39 of 8 and 10. So having us be short

5:05:42 staffed during those hours um would

5:05:44 really compromise our ability to

5:05:46 effectively serve the public. And that's

5:05:48 why we uh are I am uh very hesitant to

5:05:52 have half staff essentially run at that

5:05:54 time. There's a requirement because uh

5:05:57 of the union uh work that's done by

5:06:00 staff at the front of house that it

5:06:02 needs to be covered by union staff. Uh

5:06:04 my office has three and a half uh union

5:06:07 staff uh currently assigned to it. So if

5:06:10 you were to have two of those come in

5:06:11 late uh to cover the evening hours, you

5:06:13 would be essentially running one and a

5:06:15 half person covering the front counter

5:06:17 um which uh is a very low number for the

5:06:20 workload that we see especially during

5:06:21 the morning hours. And in terms of

5:06:24 sorry, just my last question. In terms

5:06:26 of the traffic to uh the clerk's office,

5:06:29 do you formally track this? Is it

5:06:31 anecdotal?

5:06:33 Uh we don't have formal tracking

5:06:35 numbers. Uh much of it is anecdotal. Um

5:06:37 we could certainly pull numbers such as

5:06:39 if there's uh date stamps on the

5:06:41 transactions that go through. Um but for

5:06:43 the most part, basically from when staff

5:06:45 clock in until lunch, uh what they are

5:06:47 handling is front of counter and phone

5:06:49 call work. For the most part, we aren't

5:06:51 able to shift staff over to back of

5:06:53 housework until after the lunch break

5:06:55 because that's when we do see a lull in

5:06:57 traffic. So I will say anecdotally as

5:06:59 the general operations of the clerk's

5:07:00 office things like uh you know going and

5:07:03 processing a large number of dog

5:07:04 licenses that came in online that work

5:07:06 is generally done in the afternoon

5:07:08 because that's when we see a lightning

5:07:09 of the foot traffic that's actually

5:07:11 coming into the clerk's office while

5:07:13 that foot traffic is much heavier uh in

5:07:15 the morning and typically doesn't allow

5:07:16 staff uh to come away from the counter

5:07:18 or come away from the phones.

5:07:20 Thank you. Any other questions for

5:07:22 I have a couple of comments I'd like to

5:07:23 add. I will go to you eventually. Just

5:07:25 letting everyone ask Ben just people had

5:07:26 an opportunity to ask you questions. Go

5:07:28 ahead.

5:07:28 My my question is whether your idea of

5:07:31 having people come in late um and and

5:07:34 then work in the evening is outside of

5:07:36 the scope of this article. I this just

5:07:39 says town clerk office will offer at

5:07:42 least two additional office hours. Um

5:07:46 well so I'm not I'm not sure it's even

5:07:48 relevant, but maybe it is. I I'm not

5:07:50 proposing specific textual changes to

5:07:53 the Warrant article, just trying to

5:07:54 understand the different ways that I get

5:07:56 the clerk's office could fulfill the

5:07:58 terms of the article as written.

5:08:00 Yeah,

5:08:01 I think David's line of questioning

5:08:04 lends credence to the idea of passing

5:08:07 the supporting the resolution, allowing
