5:08:11 could possibly logistically accomplish

5:08:13 it. Um, and then come back to us and be

5:08:15 like, "Hey, this is how I could do it if

5:08:17 you really want it." Um,

5:08:20 But that isn't our option to say no

5:08:23 action on the on the warrant article.

5:08:27 Yeah. On the warrant or on the on the on

5:08:29 the bylaw version. Yeah.

5:08:33 Which one is before us or on both?

5:08:36 Both are before you? There are competing

5:08:38 motions.

5:08:39 All right. Regina, you had some more

5:08:40 comments.

5:08:40 Yes. Um, a couple of you heard me say

5:08:43 this, but there were two new people here

5:08:44 and they may not have heard me say this.

5:08:47 Um, I went out and spoke at length with

5:08:50 former town clerk Pat Ward, who for

5:08:53 30some years had the evening hours. He

5:08:57 did not think it was a problem. He said

5:08:59 that it cost him overtime for about two

5:09:03 months

5:09:04 and then he made it a condition of

5:09:06 employment and they rotated so that the

5:09:09 staff would rotate as to which one

5:09:12 stayed at the counter. And of course,

5:09:13 the town clerk or his assistant has to

5:09:16 usually be there for any questions that

5:09:19 the staff is not always trained to

5:09:21 answer, as many of us know. So, he did

5:09:24 not see that as a big cost the way we're

5:09:28 hearing it here because it it wouldn't

5:09:30 end up being uh time and a half later.

5:09:34 Did Did he give you any indication as to

5:09:36 utilization of those evening hours?

5:09:39 Um he said it was very busy and and

5:09:41 several people town meeting members

5:09:44 because town meetings were not done on

5:09:46 Zoom as they consistently seem to be now

5:09:49 but in fact every night of the week

5:09:50 there was something going on at in

5:09:53 person at town hall. Many of us use

5:09:55 those services. Um I have found that

5:09:58 when I've gone in even during the day to

5:10:01 see if they if I could look up meetings

5:10:04 unfortunately staff had not kept a book.

5:10:07 They didn't know what I was talking

5:10:08 about, but it used to be kept all the

5:10:10 time at the counter and you could tell

5:10:11 what meetings were on that day, what

5:10:13 time they were, etc., etc. So, that was

5:10:16 one of the main things that many of us

5:10:17 used used it for. Um, but ultimately,

5:10:21 this is a service to the public. It

5:10:23 would need time to catch up on it, but

5:10:26 ultimately

5:10:28 um we need to be able to provide a

5:10:31 service. And if every other community is

5:10:33 doing it, and we always did it very

5:10:36 successfully and it was within budget,

5:10:39 why are we not even considering doing

5:10:42 it? At least the clerk has made it very

5:10:44 clear he doesn't want to do it.

5:10:45 Michael,

5:10:46 um I'm wondering in your conversations

5:10:48 with the clerk whether um whether you uh

5:10:54 established of the services that were

5:10:57 being offered and people took advantage

5:10:58 of the in the evening. How many of those

5:11:01 services are currently available online?

5:11:06 I'm not sure, but I'm the worst person

5:11:08 to say go online. I don't believe in it.

5:11:11 I think that we should make it for I

5:11:14 have constituents who don't have a

5:11:15 computer

5:11:17 and I I have to call one in particular

5:11:20 to tell her anything because she she is

5:11:22 adamant about not having one. So, I

5:11:25 mean, you have to to go to the least

5:11:27 common denominator for some things when

5:11:29 it's a public good. And I think in this

5:11:31 case, allowing people to be able to have

5:11:34 their job and be able to show up for

5:11:37 only two hours. The clerk, the previous

5:11:39 clerk had it for three hours every

5:11:41 Thursday night. So, I've only asked for

5:11:43 a couple because when I researched the

5:11:45 other communities, they were all

5:11:46 offering only two hours. And I thought

5:11:49 I'd stay within that, not go back to

5:11:51 Pat's original three hours. Thank you.

5:11:54 Yeah.

5:11:56 Any further questions,

5:11:58 comments?

5:12:00 I would just jump in. I I want to jump

5:12:02 in on behalf of Ben because he is a

5:12:05 department head, but he's different than

5:12:07 other department heads. So, he's more

5:12:08 constrained in terms some he's free in

5:12:10 some ways, but he's constrained in

5:12:11 others. He's elected um as opposed to

5:12:13 appointed, but he is a department head,

5:12:15 which means in some ways he answers to

5:12:17 the structure of the select board

5:12:19 office. Um I just really worry about a

5:12:22 slippery slope here. I don't think we

5:12:24 should be legislating the hours that

5:12:25 offices are open. That's a core

5:12:27 operational function. Um the town clerk

5:12:29 is elected to set those hours. People

5:12:32 disagree with those hours, they can vote

5:12:33 for a different town clerk.

5:12:38 So my position on this is I am uh very

5:12:43 supportive of the gap that the

5:12:45 petitioner is trying to fill. However, I

5:12:49 agree with the town clerk that there are

5:12:51 various ways that need further

5:12:54 exploration to figure out what would be

5:12:57 the most sensible approach to fill that

5:12:59 gap if we decide to move in that

5:13:01 direction. So I am more comfortable with

5:13:04 the advisory committee's version of

5:13:06 article 13 that essentially expresses

5:13:09 support for the sentiment behind this

5:13:12 warrant article but would not create

5:13:14 spec would not specifically legislate it

5:13:16 to uh the town administrator's point. Uh

5:13:20 so I would like to move favorable action

5:13:23 on the advisory committee version of

5:13:25 warrant article 13 and this one we

5:13:27 haven't taken up so no need for

5:13:28 reconsideration first. Any further

5:13:30 discussion?

5:13:33 All right, Bernard.

5:13:34 No, because I'd like to say no action on

5:13:37 both of them, but so no.

5:13:39 Okay,

5:13:40 Michael,

5:13:43 come back to me.

5:13:45 Oh, we can do that. Amanda,

5:13:48 no.

5:13:49 All right, Anthony. And I also vote yes.

5:13:54 So my you know, that's the that's the

5:13:56 downside of this.

5:14:02 Um,

5:14:04 I'm going to vote no.

5:14:06 All right.

5:14:07 If that passes, as you just voted,

5:14:10 you'll be the only town in the greater

5:14:12 128 area that doesn't offer evening

5:14:14 hours. Keep that in mind, please.

5:14:17 So, um, can I just clarify, David? Um,

5:14:20 you vote the the motion to support the

5:14:23 AC motion failed, but I still need the

5:14:25 board to take a position. So if you um I

5:14:28 think Bernard is intending on moving no

5:14:30 action. I just want to make sure I'm

5:14:31 following.

5:14:32 I move no action on the warrant article

5:14:35 uh 13 uh in its original form as a

5:14:39 bylaw.

5:14:40 All right. Bernard,

5:14:41 yes.

5:14:43 Michael,

5:14:43 yes.

5:14:44 Amanda,

5:14:45 yes.

5:14:46 Anthony,

5:14:46 yes.

5:14:47 I also vote yes.

5:14:50 All right. With that, we will move on to

5:14:54 warrant article 22. the proposition two

5:14:57 and a half resolution and we have uh one

5:15:00 of the co-etitioners here, Bradford

5:15:02 Kimble.

5:15:03 You have the other one online.

5:15:05 All right, we have both.

5:15:06 Yep.

5:15:08 Good evening everybody. Uh Bradford

5:15:10 Kimble, TMM precinct 1 and one of the

5:15:13 co-petitioners of Warren Article 22

5:15:15 along with Rob Shown uh TMM1 who's on

5:15:18 Zoom as well. Uh we came to the select

5:15:21 board a couple of weeks ago. Um, but I'd

5:15:23 like to give sort of a a re resummarize

5:15:26 what our warrant article does, why we

5:15:27 think it's a good idea, and sort of

5:15:29 where we stand. Um, so our warrant

5:15:31 article, warrant article 22, as amended

5:15:34 on the combined as available on the

5:15:36 combined reports 22-6, calls for the

5:15:38 select board to create a new town

5:15:40 committee that would study the

5:15:41 feasibility and possibility of creating

5:15:44 what we call a municipal core services

5:15:46 cost index. essentially a single

5:15:48 statistic that would track the effect

5:15:50 that would track the effect of inflation

5:15:52 on the town's budgets whether it comes

5:15:54 from uh employee compensation, capital

5:15:58 costs, utilities increases, uh etc. And

5:16:00 so we think this is a good idea because

5:16:03 this singular metric would show the gap

5:16:05 between cost growth and existing

5:16:06 services. And the proposition 2 and a

5:16:08 half levy cap improved override

5:16:10 discussion providing a real helpful

5:16:12 benchmark and singular statistic that

5:16:14 would help uh that would help with

5:16:16 transparency enables comparison to peer

5:16:18 communities and provides an important

5:16:20 data source for any discussions about

5:16:21 Proposition 2 and a half in town and

5:16:24 also regionally and beyond statewide.

5:16:26 Um, and we also think it fits really

5:16:28 well with sort of the select board

5:16:29 2030's roadmap commitment to

5:16:31 transparency, having the singular metric

5:16:33 that tracks the effect of inflation on

5:16:35 the budget. Um,

5:16:37 so that's sort of uh where we are.

5:16:39 That's the that's the summary. And uh my

5:16:42 co-partitioner Rob Shown met with the

5:16:43 town administrator this afternoon. Thank

5:16:45 you for for doing that. So he might be

5:16:47 better equipped to answer some questions

5:16:48 that arose in that meeting as well. Um

5:16:51 we also want to specify that this

5:16:53 committee's charge would be limited and

5:16:54 specific. It's to create a singular

5:16:57 metric and we think that this committee

5:16:58 could do it and that the town and

5:17:00 townwide town citizens have the

5:17:02 capability to produce this metric with

5:17:04 hopefully minimal effort from town

5:17:05 staff. Though we acknowledge that the

5:17:07 town administrator um has some concerns

5:17:09 about that as well.

5:17:12 All right. Any questions or comments

5:17:15 from board members? Anthony. Only

5:17:17 comment I have is I love the idea as

5:17:19 someone who reads the CPI prints the

5:17:22 minute they come out from the Bureau of

5:17:24 Labor Statistics and is genuinely

5:17:26 excited for non non-farm payroll stats.

5:17:28 Any municipal indices like this uh are

5:17:30 right up my alley. I believe they're

5:17:32 super important for good government. Um

5:17:34 I love quantitative metrics like this

5:17:35 that helps us communicate the growth of

5:17:38 costs. Um and I think it's really a good

5:17:40 way to bring the discussion of Prop 2

5:17:43 and a half up to the focal point. And uh

5:17:45 I think it's a great idea.

5:17:47 All right. So, at when we last discussed

5:17:49 this Warren article, there were some

5:17:51 concerns about staff bandwidth for uh

5:17:56 interacting with the tracking of this

5:17:58 with those who would be monitoring the

5:17:59 tracking of this. Could you comment more

5:18:01 on that?

5:18:01 Yeah, I just I I think and I agree that

5:18:04 it's a value it would be this is a the

5:18:06 idea is valuable. Um the idea is

5:18:09 valuable. The aims of this are laudable.

5:18:12 to try and you know get some get some

5:18:15 data and use it to good ends. Um the

5:18:19 concerns that I have are one I do think

5:18:21 that it would require more staff time

5:18:23 than the petitioners think. Um it's

5:18:25 almost always the case. It's not it's

5:18:27 not their fault. I but I think it is the

5:18:29 reality of the situation that when you

5:18:30 create a committee you have two choices.

5:18:33 You can let the committee do its own

5:18:34 thing which can result in the committee

5:18:36 then getting away from you and doing

5:18:38 something that you may not want it to

5:18:40 do. or you can staff it. Um, and that

5:18:43 requires someone to be at every meeting.

5:18:45 That requires someone to to keep people

5:18:48 on task. That requires going back and

5:18:50 forth on this. Um, and you know, it just

5:18:55 creates a situation where I'm going to

5:18:57 have to send someone to this committee's

5:19:00 meetings and do the kind of work

5:19:04 necessary to ensure that the committee

5:19:06 is accomplishing the goals that you've

5:19:08 set it out to accomplish. Um, and I

5:19:12 don't know who that person would be,

5:19:13 frankly. Um, and I

5:19:18 think that

5:19:21 the and the other kind of concern that I

5:19:23 have on this one is kind of to what end?

5:19:25 Um, as I was saying to Rob who and we

5:19:28 had a really good conversation about

5:19:29 this, the one of the co-etitioners, um,

5:19:31 ironically, Brookline to some people on

5:19:34 the outside is a success story of Prop 2

5:19:37 and a half. Rline passes overrides on a

5:19:40 regular basis by wide margins. We are

5:19:42 proof that the system works to them. Um

5:19:44 if a community wants higher taxes, they

5:19:46 can vote for higher taxes. So if our

5:19:49 goal in this is to change the perception

5:19:52 of Prop 2 and a half and to demonstrate

5:19:55 that it exacerbates the tension between

5:19:57 the halves and the have nots, you know,

5:19:59 I'm very grateful to the voters for what

5:20:01 they did to to fund our municipal

5:20:04 budget. I think it is a huge vote of

5:20:06 confidence in local government. I think

5:20:08 it makes a huge difference in the

5:20:09 community. It's just it it is it is a

5:20:12 really heartwarming story. But then I

5:20:14 compare it to Malden where the need was

5:20:17 acute and the vote was not there. And I

5:20:19 compare it to Chelsea where they can't

5:20:21 even do it. You know, where the concept

5:20:24 of asking the people who live in Chelsea

5:20:26 for more money is just impossible. Um,

5:20:29 and I say that's,

5:20:34 you know, if if all we're doing is

5:20:35 indexing in Brooklyn, we're not really

5:20:38 providing information that's going to

5:20:40 change the minds of the people whose

5:20:42 minds need to be changed on this. I

5:20:45 would rather, if we were to do this,

5:20:48 work on building a regional index. I

5:20:50 would rather work with the met

5:20:53 Metropolitan Area Planning Council um

5:20:55 who run the Metro Mayor's Association

5:20:58 which is now up to I think 18 members

5:21:00 and it includes a diverse array a basket

5:21:02 of communities. It includes Chelsea, it

5:21:04 includes Malden, it includes Everett, it

5:21:06 includes Lynn, it includes Arlington, it

5:21:09 includes Brainree, it includes Boston.

5:21:11 Um, and I think the conversation that

5:21:16 we've had in those in those meetings

5:21:18 about Prop 2 and a half and what could

5:21:20 be done to address it at the regional

5:21:22 level um have been productive. Um, and I

5:21:25 would be happy to take the subject of

5:21:27 this and bring it to that body and say,

5:21:29 "Hey guys, like we pay we pay dues to be

5:21:32 part of this organization. Um, can we

5:21:34 work together and and build this index

5:21:36 on a regional level?" Um, but I think

5:21:39 just the information from Brooklyn is

5:21:42 not necessarily going to be helpful in

5:21:44 the broader discussion. And I don't

5:21:46 necessarily think on the education

5:21:48 level, although I think Rob, you know,

5:21:50 Rob can prove me wrong on this, but the

5:21:55 lesson that I took from this election is

5:21:56 that it was a turnout election. Um, and

5:21:59 that I don't think a lot of people's

5:22:00 minds were changed on the doorstep about

5:22:03 the override. Um, I think it was a

5:22:05 question of getting the base out. And in

5:22:07 this instance, the base that was gotten

5:22:09 out was a base that was very pro-

5:22:11 override. Um, and again, I'm grateful

5:22:15 for that. But I don't necessarily know

5:22:17 how our the benefit the costbenefit

5:22:20 analysis of getting that additional

5:22:22 information out there is going to change

5:22:24 people's minds because I think the

5:22:25 conversation is still going to be the

5:22:27 same that it's always been since Prop 2

5:22:28 and a half was passed, which is I don't

5:22:30 want my property taxes to go up. This is

5:22:32 all I have. or I need property taxes to

5:22:34 go up because this is how we fund the

5:22:36 services. So, I see that uh

5:22:38 co-patitioner Rob Shown has his hand up.

5:22:40 Go ahead.

5:22:41 Yeah, just I I don't think CHZ takes

5:22:44 enough credit for the job that that he

5:22:46 and other people did in communicating

5:22:49 ahead of the election the benefits of

5:22:52 voting yes. And I know personally I I

5:22:54 told Chad Chad Chaz this is I had just

5:22:57 had conversations with friends of mine

5:22:59 that were firm no voters and by

5:23:02 explaining this inflation issue I was

5:23:05 able to change I believe I mean I wasn't

5:23:07 in their in the voting booth with them

5:23:09 but I feel like I did manage to change

5:23:11 some minds. So, I'm thinking ahead to

5:23:13 like, you know, we're probably going to

5:23:14 have future override votes and I feel

5:23:16 like this would be a tool that would be

5:23:19 really helpful in future override

5:23:21 discussions in Brooklyn. And then the

5:23:23 other thing I would say is that while,

5:23:25 you know, I was surprised that the the

5:23:27 margin that the override did pass by,

5:23:30 you know, I feel like it still was a

5:23:32 very divisive vote. I mean, we still had

5:23:35 what, 40% people vote no. And you know,

5:23:39 there's a lot of people in town that are

5:23:41 are still kind of sore about, you know,

5:23:44 about the override. And so, you know, I

5:23:47 would like to do if there's something we

5:23:49 can do to change some of their minds by

5:23:51 helping them explain the inflation

5:23:53 component of these budget situations. I

5:23:57 think that would be a great thing.

5:23:59 Go ahead.

5:24:00 Yeah, I think uh addressing the town

5:24:02 administrator's first point, uh this

5:24:04 committee, right, is is not necessarily

5:24:05 intended to be a long-lasting body. It

5:24:07 has a very specific charge, the creation

5:24:09 of one metric, you know, which could be

5:24:11 broken down into several metrics, but it

5:24:13 doesn't advocate or make specific

5:24:14 recommendations outside of that specific

5:24:16 mandate. So, it's extremely limited in

5:24:18 scope to this one metric that we think

5:24:20 would be extremely useful. Uh the second

5:24:22 point regarding sort of um objections

5:24:27 regarding the override and also regional

5:24:28 and versus local distinctions is that we

5:24:30 envision this committee as being a a

5:24:33 combination of starting local

5:24:35 conversations that that educate voters

5:24:37 and educate the town and educate

5:24:38 decision makers in town but also

5:24:40 starting this starting the step for

5:24:42 creating sort of regional change. We

5:24:43 don't necessarily think that this

5:24:44 committee is a political tool, but it is

5:24:47 also true that the Massachusetts

5:24:49 Municipal Association's recommendations

5:24:51 for reforming Prop 2 and a half all

5:24:53 essentially rely on the decisions of

5:24:55 municipalities opting in. Whether it's

5:24:57 multi-year overrides or whether it's

5:25:00 having municipalities opt in to an

5:25:02 adjustable levy cap, these are likely

5:25:04 not recommendations that would be passed

5:25:06 in sweeping fashion statewide, but

5:25:07 recommendations that could be adopted by

5:25:09 individual municipalities. So if

5:25:12 Brooklyn has this index, it would

5:25:13 provide a really strong foundation to

5:25:15 see how how does one municipality sort

5:25:17 of tackle the effect that inflationary

5:25:19 pressures have on the budget. And then

5:25:21 the second part about voter education is

5:25:23 that I think a common objection to the

5:25:25 override was why not just cut waste? Why

5:25:28 not cut discretionary spending? And I

5:25:29 think this metric would help voters

5:25:31 understand what is discretionary

5:25:33 spending and then what is re what are

5:25:35 the cost drivers that are that come from

5:25:37 inflation. And there's a there's a

5:25:38 difference there.

5:25:40 Michael.

5:25:41 Um,

5:25:43 so first of all, thank you both of you

5:25:46 uh for bringing this question forward.

5:25:49 Um, and you had mentioned before, you

5:25:51 know, the issues of transparency in the

5:25:53 road map. Um, and I'm one of the things

5:25:56 that I'm a little bit concerned about in

5:25:59 the way that you've you've sort of

5:26:01 structured the Warren article at this

5:26:03 point is that you know there's a set of

5:26:06 data that you need in order to create

5:26:10 this index. Um but but in terms of of

5:26:15 sort of the message of the index, you

5:26:17 know, I think what what the town

5:26:19 administrator is saying is that that the

5:26:21 message that you will get is relatively

5:26:24 well understood and can be reasonably

5:26:26 well articulated. So I'm I am concerned

5:26:30 that by setting up this index as

5:26:33 something that we maintain over time

5:26:35 that the cost of maintenance over time

5:26:38 will uh overtake the value of producing

5:26:42 that index you know in order to you know

5:26:46 establish the argument that you're

5:26:48 trying to make. Um and so one of the

5:26:51 things that I am wondering about is uh

5:26:54 and I and I don't expect an answer right

5:26:57 now but I'm wondering whether there is

5:26:59 some way to separate the question of

5:27:03 what is the data that the town needs to

5:27:06 expose in order to produce this

5:27:10 particular index and is there a way of

5:27:14 uh of exposing or releasing or sharing

5:27:17 that information

5:27:20 such that it is generally available not

5:27:23 only for this index but for the

5:27:25 potential of other transparency ideas

5:27:28 that are that are brought forth by the

5:27:31 public.

5:27:33 I think the one the one area here where

5:27:35 you're going to have the biggest problem

5:27:36 is with the schools. The schools are

5:27:38 going to have to play ball with this.

5:27:40 Um, and one of the biggest drivers of

5:27:43 school costs, as David knows, is special

5:27:48 education and transportation,

5:27:50 particularly for medically fragile

5:27:51 students. Um, and I think you will run

5:27:54 into a great deal of resistance from the

5:27:56 school committee to give you that data

5:27:59 because the school committee will say

5:28:01 there are privacy issues involved even

5:28:03 at the statistical level. And um it

5:28:07 creates this idea that special education

5:28:10 is something they're you're going to see

5:28:11 all the numbers and you're going to see

5:28:12 special education volatile

5:28:14 year-over-year but generally going up

5:28:16 sometimes by double digits even as a

5:28:19 result potentially of one student moving

5:28:20 into the district. And people are going

5:28:22 to say well there's your waste. That's

5:28:25 not waste. That's a kid you know that's

5:28:27 someone who needs to be cared for. Um I

5:28:30 worry that that that you when you talk

5:28:33 about the data that is to be provided

5:28:34 that that's an area where you may wind

5:28:37 up inadvertently

5:28:39 um needing to do remedial education um

5:28:42 because when you see numbers go up by

5:28:45 that I eyepopping amount or down by that

5:28:47 eyepopping amount um people say what's

5:28:50 going on here um and maybe that is

5:28:52 helpful in the long run um but you know

5:28:55 I've just you know I I I I

5:29:00 want us to be mindful of the fact that

5:29:02 we are required to provide everyone with

5:29:04 a free and appropriate public education.

5:29:06 Um and that sometimes means spending a

5:29:08 lot of money. Um so it's I I think the

5:29:11 school you'd run into resistance from

5:29:12 the schools when it comes to what needs

5:29:14 to be given. And I think that then does

5:29:17 unnecess does potentially make this more

5:29:20 of a political project, right? You know,

5:29:22 how are we how can we get information

5:29:24 from the schools? Why aren't the schools

5:29:25 giving us this information? If the

5:29:26 schools want an override, they better

5:29:28 give us this information. Um, I could

5:29:30 see that getting ugly.

5:29:33 Yeah. I mean, actually, it's not just

5:29:37 the schools. I mean, it's the town, too.

5:29:39 I mean, our procurement rules increase

5:29:41 cost of of everything, and that creates

5:29:45 sort of a political uh situation where

5:29:47 people think that uh there's waste in

5:29:50 town services.

5:29:51 Yeah. But I I think that's easier to

5:29:52 explain explain though. I think you know

5:29:54 and you know we when we were on

5:29:56 expenditures and revenues we did this

5:29:58 exercise on the town side. I think Dick

5:29:59 Ben did a really good job of presenting

5:30:01 for example here's all the town budgets

5:30:03 that went up since last override by more

5:30:05 than two and a half%. Can I can I

5:30:06 understand why? And we had to answer

5:30:08 that question. It was a good question to

5:30:10 ask and it was a good exercise for us to

5:30:12 be able to answer that and say yep this

5:30:14 went up by this amount for this reason.

5:30:16 Um and you could see that when you did

5:30:18 that exercise that there wasn't waste or

5:30:21 bloat. there wasn't just, you know,

5:30:22 adding positions that the budgets went

5:30:25 up for good reasons, but when you looked

5:30:27 at them from numbers on a page, it might

5:30:29 have looked like, whoa, what's what's

5:30:30 that huge increase doing there? So,

5:30:32 building an building an index that

5:30:35 accurately reflects

5:30:37 um you know the the costs, but then also

5:30:40 does the work of educating um is is

5:30:42 tough.

5:30:44 Not impossible, but is is going to take

5:30:47 some doing. Could you explain why you

5:30:49 see value to this exercise at a regional

5:30:51 level rather than just at the hyper

5:30:54 local? I think that then impacts it then

5:30:56 demonstrates that it is not just rich

5:30:59 towns, poor towns, towns that are towns

5:31:02 that have commercial development, towns

5:31:03 that don't have commercial development.

5:31:05 This is a problem across the

5:31:07 Commonwealth and it's going to require I

5:31:11 solutions I I think what the legislature

5:31:13 is going to say to those opt-in

5:31:15 solutions is that exacerbates a have and

5:31:16 have not problem. It's the same reason

5:31:18 why they don't allow us to raise the

5:31:20 meal tax and the hotel tax. That's why

5:31:21 they're not letting us do the governor's

5:31:24 um proposals on um municipal um uh x

5:31:29 taxes um because they know that there

5:31:32 are communities that have that stuff and

5:31:33 communities that don't. um and they

5:31:35 don't want us to be continually leaping

5:31:37 out ahead. But if we're able to

5:31:39 demonstrate that no, there is a through

5:31:40 line here. Doesn't matter if you're

5:31:42 Brainree or or Brookline, um you we the

5:31:46 problems we we experience are universal.

5:51:21 Um I I just had a question about the

5:51:22 fingerprinting thing. If the if the

5:51:24 federal government requests data from us

5:51:27 that we currently have,

5:51:29 aren't we legally obligated to give that

5:51:31 data?

5:51:32 In most cases, not all. Um, it depends

5:51:34 on for what purpose. You know, we don't

5:51:36 share agree. We don't share data with

5:51:37 ICE, right?

5:51:38 But but we don't proactively share data

5:51:40 with ICE,

5:51:41 right?

5:51:41 If they request data from us, isn't it

5:51:43 like federally mandated that we have to

5:51:45 give data

5:51:45 if they subpoena it or if they if

5:51:47 there's a court order? Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

5:51:49 So when they say we can't share

5:51:51 fingerprints, isn't that like

5:51:52 immediately that's just illegal?

5:51:54 Well, what they're what they're asking

5:51:55 for is is more nuanced than that. It is

5:51:58 this it's right now if you are arrested,

5:52:00 you're booked, you're fingerprinted, and

5:52:02 when you're fingerprinted, that is those

5:52:03 your fingerprints are run through a

5:52:05 database. And that d and when that

5:52:07 database is run, it triggers hits. And

5:52:09 one of the things that it triggers hits

5:52:11 in is ICE's containers. And so whenever

5:52:14 that happens, and this has happened now

5:52:15 six times in the last year, we get a

5:52:17 phone call from ICE saying, "Hey, you

5:52:20 guys, you guys have someone who has a

5:52:21 civil detainer out. Please hold that

5:52:23 person until we get there." And we say,

5:52:25 "No, we do not cooperate with you. Do

5:52:28 not do that. Goodbye." We hang up the

5:52:29 phone.

5:52:30 A followup on that. You said six times

5:52:32 it happened.

5:52:33 Yes.

5:52:33 In the last year.

5:52:34 In the last year. Yeah.

5:52:39 uh of those six people?

5:56:27 that's an area too where I feel like

5:56:30 there's been there's been a lot of

5:56:31 effort to explain what YASI is and what

5:56:34 it does and why how we spend it and why

5:56:36 it exists. And the response that I feel

5:56:40 well and good but also funds the brick

5:56:42 and therefore is inherently tainted and

5:56:44 we shouldn't take it. Um and I just

5:56:47 don't agree with that. Anthony,

5:56:50 um could we just do a quick overview of

5:56:51 the current ICE uh cooperation policy

5:56:54 that we have because I went to the

5:56:56 select board meeting where we where you

5:56:58 guys discussed the uh issuance of

5:57:00 general order 43, I believe, and I spoke

5:57:03 on behalf of passing that general order

5:57:05 or reaffirming it that was originally

5:57:07 passed in 2017. And can we go through

5:57:10 that again and just specify what that

5:57:12 does on the record here tonight?

5:57:13 Sure. So, General Order 43 in the police

5:57:15 department says we do not participate in

5:57:18 civil immigration enforcement in any

5:57:19 way. We do not participate in raids. We

5:57:21 do not particip we do not provide

5:57:23 material support to civil immigration

5:57:25 enforcement in any way. Um, we don't do

5:57:28 any of that. Um, you know, we will abide

5:57:31 by a valid judicial order. Um, so if

5:57:34 someone shows up with a court order, not

5:57:36 an administrative order, and says you

5:57:38 have to do you have to provide us with

5:57:39 X, then we will do it. We will follow a

5:57:41 lawfully issued subpoena. But we're not

5:57:44 cooperating with ICE or any, you know,

5:57:46 CBP or any of those folks in civil

5:57:49 immigration enforcement in any way.

5:57:51 And that basis of general order 43 is

5:57:53 perfectly in line with like the

5:57:54 anti-comandeering doctrine that goes all

5:57:56 the way back to like the fugitive slave

5:57:58 act of like northern states not wanting

5:58:00 to catch uh runaway slaves. It's the

5:58:03 same exact principle at stake here. So

5:58:06 what is the petitioner's I'm I'm just

5:58:08 trying to understand that what the

5:58:09 petitioner is asking for is I see a lot

5:58:11 of resolution demands at the state

5:58:14 level. I see a lot of that and then I

5:58:16 see the the other two organizations that

5:58:20 you brought up and what is I'm trying to

5:58:22 understand the direct benefit

5:58:24 of of the of that on our immigrant

5:58:27 community.

5:58:28 Yeah. So the the brick is a regional

5:58:30 intelligence gathering group. It's was

5:58:33 founded in the aftermath of the Boston

5:58:35 Marathon bombing and it is designed to

5:58:37 ensure that you know we have a bunch of

5:58:38 little municipalities surrounding a big

5:58:40 municipality. It's designed to ensure

5:58:42 that we are sharing regional

5:58:43 intelligence and that we're all up to

5:58:45 date on what's going on and that we were

5:58:46 able to crosscoordinate. I mean that

5:58:48 shooting in Cambridge, right? Like we

5:58:50 got an immediate warning from the brick

5:58:51 on that and they were, you know, hugely

5:58:55 responsible for a lot of that

5:58:56 coordination in terms of effort there,

5:58:58 coordinating between the state and MIMA

5:59:00 and the Massachusetts emergency

5:59:02 management and so forth. Um so the brick

5:59:07 with that said the when the brick was

5:59:10 founded um there were concerns that they

5:59:14 were keeping a database that it was easy

5:59:16 to get into and hard to get out of

5:59:18 right where if you were um associated

5:59:23 with a gang then brick would put you in

5:59:25 a database and that would mean that you

5:59:26 were eligible to be surveiled that you

5:59:29 were you know that there were there were

5:59:31 more um that that you were subject to

5:59:34 heightened scrutiny. Um, and there was

5:59:36 nothing you could do about it.

5:59:37 And the criteria for being a gang member

5:59:39 was very loosey goosey.

5:59:40 Right. Right. They did not have strict

5:59:42 restrictions on who, you know, how to

5:59:44 how to determine who was a gang member.

5:59:46 They did not there was no due process

5:59:48 involved in it. Um, you know, you often

5:59:51 didn't know that you were in the

5:59:52 database until they picked you up. Um,

5:59:55 they're like, "Yeah, we have you on

5:59:56 suspicion of gang activity." Um and um

6:00:01 ultimately um there were you know there

6:00:05 was a very serious situation in which

6:00:07 someone was held on suspicion of being a

6:00:08 gang member and was charged with crime.

6:00:11 Um there were immigration there were

6:00:13 immigration consequences for it. Um it

6:00:15 really was a pretty devastating case and

6:00:18 ultimately the state or the federal

6:00:21 courts were very um uh damning of the

6:00:25 bricks operations in that regard. Um,

6:00:27 and as a result of that, Boston passed a

6:00:30 number of laws, including the Trust Act

6:00:32 in 2019 that was basically designed to

6:00:36 reform the brick and bring it into

6:00:40 compliance with these requirements.

6:00:43 Since then, I understand that the ACLU

6:00:46 and other organizations still feel like

6:00:48 the brick does not have adequate

6:00:49 oversight. And I think that's what the

6:00:51 petitioners I mean I'm are are arguing

6:00:53 is that the brick still does not have

6:00:55 the amount of oversight that they uh

6:00:58 that they need that they're still not

6:00:59 being fully disclosing of of all of

6:01:01 this. Um what I will say though is that

6:01:04 since then there have been discussions

6:01:06 at Boston's level about leaving the

6:01:07 break. At one point the city council did

6:01:10 I oh no sorry that was the city council

6:01:11 voted to stop accepting Yuasi briefly

6:01:13 and then reversed itself. Um but you

6:01:16 know there have been discussions about

6:01:19 the brick at Boston at the Boston level

6:01:22 since it's their center and we just we

6:01:25 share it with them

6:01:26 and they have not taken those steps.

6:01:30 They have definitely held hearings. They

6:01:31 have called for greater oversight but

6:01:33 the mayor has not called for the brick

6:01:34 to be abolished. Um and the mayor just

6:01:37 got reelected. Um so I think there's a

6:01:40 strong argument to be made here to

6:01:42 Bernard's point. oversight is critical

6:01:45 and we are I don't think there's
